data.title: Starmer pledges to spend billions more on drugs in NHS overhaul
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer is preparing to boost NHS spending on drugs to prevent an exodus of investment from the UK.

The Government is set to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. The move will allow the NHS to pay billions of pounds more for medicines.

Several drug companies have scrapped investments in the UK amid a row over how much the Government was spending on new drugs compared to other countries.

The pricing shake-up will appease drugmakers – but it also threatens to put Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, on a collision course with Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, over how to fund the multibillion-pound bill.

Lord Vallance, the science minister – who is negotiating a new deal with the industry on behalf of the Government – admitted last month that price increases were necessary to stop a wave of investment leaving the UK.

The Times reported that the Treasury is insisting that spending increases must be funded through existing departmental budgets, while NHS bosses are demanding extra financial support to pay for any deal.

The looming showdown comes as the Chancellor prepares to unveil another tax-raising Budget, with economists predicting extra levies of around £30bn to balance the books.

Ms Reeves boosted health spending at the expense of other government departments in her June spending review, with the department accounting for 90pc of the extra money for public services over the next three years.

It means the NHS and other health spending is now on course to account for half of all spending on public services by the end of the decade.

Meanwhile, a string of companies including Merck, the US cancer drug manufacturer, and Eli Lily, maker of obesity jab Mounjaro, have halted investment in the UK over the past year in protest against the NHS’s drug pricing regime.

This week, AstraZeneca’s UK chairman signalled that the company was ready to scrap investment in Britain unless the Government backed down on pricing.

Shaun Grady warned that the UK was in danger of becoming unattractive for companies unless it brings its “appalling” drugs regime into the 21st century.

Pharmaceutical companies are also under pressure from Donald Trump, the US president, who has escalated tensions by threatening 100pc tariffs on the sector if it does not invest in America.

The antiquated system used by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) to determine which treatments are available on the NHS has not been changed since 1999.

The system uses a formula which seeks to determine value for money based on a drug’s effectiveness.

Currently, Nice will approve medicines that cost less than £20,000-£30,000 for every extra “quality-adjusted life year”.

A new range of between £25,000-£35,000 per year is said to be one option under consideration.

However, the Association of the British Pharmaceutical Industry has warned that the threshold would be closer to £56,000 if it had climbed in line with the cost of living. It has called for a range closer to £50,000.

A government spokesman said: “The pharmaceutical sector and the innovative medicines it produces are critical to our NHS, our economy and the plan for change.

“Through our life sciences sector plan, we’ve committed to working with industry to accelerate growth in spending on innovative medicines compared to the previous decade.

“We’ve secured a landmark economic partnership with the US that includes working together on pharmaceutical exports from the UK whilst improving conditions for pharmaceutical companies here.

“We’re now in advanced discussions with the US administration to secure the best outcome for the UK, reflecting our strong relationship and the opportunities from close partnership with our pharmaceutical industry.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Iceland boss says competition rules ‘rigged’ in Aldi and Lidl’s favour
data.description:
The boss of Iceland has claimed Britain’s competition rules are rigged in favour of Aldi and Lidl amid a row over new store openings.

Richard Walker, the executive chairman of the 980-store retailer, claimed the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) was being too slow in forcing the German chains to face similar store-opening restrictions as traditional supermarkets.

Under rules introduced in 2010, Tesco, Sainsbury’s, Morrisons, Asda, Waitrose, Marks & Spencer and Co-op are prevented from blocking rival supermarkets from opening nearby their stores.

However, Aldi and Lidl are not included in the rules, meaning they can write clauses into their land contracts which stop rivals from opening nearby.

Mr Walker said the CMA ought to take urgent action, warning that the German discounters were using restrictive land clauses in their new stores to curb competition.

“The regulator drones on about being pro-growth but fails to act when markets are clearly being rigged,” he claimed.

“I urge the CMA to pick up the phone to me today to tell me I am wrong, and that they are going to fix this mess. This has got to stop – what are they afraid of?”

Aldi and Lidl were not included in the order because when the rules came in, the pair were considered too small – holding just 3pc and 2.5pc of the grocery market respectively.

However, supermarket bosses have demanded a rethink, so that Aldi and Lidl fall under the restrictions.

Today, the two grocers hold almost a fifth of the grocery market.

Aldi is Britain’s fourth largest supermarket, opening a new store every week, while Lidl is its sixth biggest grocer. It will open its 1,000th store next week.

Iceland is also exempt from the restrictive land clauses because of its small size. However, the rules mean it too cannot expand into Aldi and Lidl’s territory.

Mr Walker claimed both companies were engaging in “anti-competitive practices to block us from opening stores near them” without facing rebuke from regulators.

Unfair playing field

It follows complaints by grocery chiefs over apparent hold-ups at the regulator in its overhaul of the rules.

Supermarket bosses first raised claims of an unfair playing field with Aldi and Lidl last autumn, with officials at the time said to have suggested the rules would be reviewed in early 2025.

In April, the CMA told supermarkets that it was aware of their frustration over the different treatment.

However, an overhaul of the system has since been pushed back multiple times, grocery insiders claim.

Until last month, companies say they had been told to expect an update in the autumn. In October, the CMA is said to have told companies that it would complete its review by the end of the year.

Supermarket sources said they now expected the CMA to “further kick the can down the road” and request more information rather than slap new restrictions on Aldi and Lidl.

One senior grocery source said the situation was preventing supermarkets from opening stores, suggesting it was “entirely inconsistent with the Government’s focus on economic growth, fair competition and consumers”.

They added that the regulator appeared “unwilling or unable to make even simple, timely tweaks to regulation to unlock retail investment”.

Rachel Reeves is already looking into whether top watchdogs, such as the CMA, have become a brake on the economy. The Chancellor has described regulators as “a boot on the neck” of businesses.

The CMA said it took retailers’ concerns seriously.

A spokesman said: “We must act within the legal framework and be fair to all grocery retailers and we are committed to using our powers to support competition.

“We expect to provide an update soon.”

data.title: Traders ramp up bets against AI darling Oracle
data.description:
Traders are ramping up bets against artificial intelligence (AI) darling Oracle by buying financial instruments intended to protect them in the event of a default.

The price of Oracle’s credit-default swaps (CDS), financial instruments used to guard against potential non-payment of debts, has spiked in recent weeks to its highest level in three years. The price of the insurance policies has more than doubled since September.

The higher the value of the swaps, the greater the premium that an investor must pay to insure themselves against the company defaulting on its debt. A credit downgrade can also result in higher premiums for buyers of these swaps.

CDSs can be used by lenders to protect against a default. However, they can also be used by traders to manage risk or bet against a particular company, given that the instruments pay out if a business defaults.

Investors have traded more than $5bn (£3.8bn) in these swaps since September, according to Jigar Patel, a Barclays analyst. That compares to just $200m last year, Bloomberg reported.

The cost of Oracle’s CDSs are far higher than that of other tech giants, according to Jim Reid, a strategist at Deutsche Bank.

“Oracle stands out dramatically,” Mr Reid said in a note to clients this week. “The last few weeks clearly mark a new phase of the AI boom – one in which investors are increasingly looking to hedge their risk.”

Shares in Oracle, which is led by Larry Ellison, 81, have plunged by a third since September as investors grow skittish about AI.

While once best known for selling arcane database tools and business software, Oracle’s market valuation soared to nearly $1tn in September after it announced a $300bn deal to provide computing power to OpenAI, the developer of ChatGPT.

It has since lost more than $300bn in value amid rising fears about a bubble in AI stocks. A series of deals that tie together Oracle, Nvidia and OpenAI have stoked concerns about overcooked AI valuations. 

Critics have argued that these apparently circular deals have artificially pushed up the values of the tech giants.

The rise in the price of its CDS comes amid reports that Oracle has been seeking to raise as much as $38bn in debt to fund AI infrastructure. It already has more than $100bn in debt on its balance sheet.

Oracle shares fell 2.5pc on Thursday, despite a wider rally across the technology sector after Nvidia revealed blockbuster results on Wednesday night.

Mr Ellison is expected to be a major buyer of Nvidia’s graphics chips. Oracle is part of OpenAI’s Project Stargate, which is proposing to build a $500bn network of vast data centres across the US.

Earlier this year, Oracle’s share price surge briefly made Mr Ellison, an ally of Donald Trump, the world’s richest man. Mr Ellison, who founded Oracle, still serves as its chief technology officer and chairman and has been a major cheerleader of the AI boom.

The Oracle chief, who is worth more than $270bn, is also a major backer of the Tony Blair Institute for Global Change, the former prime minister’s think tank, and is funding a new research institute in Oxford.

Oracle was contacted for comment.

data.title: Sadiq Khan’s Oxford Street plan ‘will fuel shoplifting and mugging’
data.description:
Sir Sadiq Khan’s bid to pedestrianise Oxford Street will turn the thoroughfare into a haven for shoplifters, muggers and criminal gangs, local residents have warned.

The Marylebone Association, which represents local residents and businesses, said that rather than creating the “world-class boulevard” touted by the London Mayor, the plan will add to soaring crime rates on Europe’s busiest shopping street.

Branding the proposals “dangerous, unworkable and catastrophic for the surrounding neighbourhoods”, the association said they would make the shopping district a “magnet” for miscreants.

The comments came as Transport for London (TfL) published details of proposed traffic diversions and revealed that bicycles will be banned under the plans.

Responding to the proposals, the association said: “The scheme solves nothing and creates new risks on an unprecedented scale.

“Removing traffic removes natural surveillance, turning the street into an open stretch vulnerable to gangs, rough sleeping, illegal trading and street crime.

“Businesses already report soaring shoplifting, assaults and anti-social behaviour. Even the Mayor’s own strategy relies on a major expansion of the late night economy, increasing alcohol licences and extensions.”

The association said that while “Oxford Street may be car-free, its neighbours will not be” with taxis, delivery vehicles and buses forced onto residential streets.

It said that Oxford Street – home to more than 300 stores including flagship branches of John Lewis, Selfridges and Marks & Spencer – will meanwhile become a “no-go zone” for elderly and disabled shoppers, who would face a walk up to 400 metres from relocated bus stops.

Also, almost half of all shops on the street have no rear access, so deliveries and rubbish collections would be pushed into side streets and need to be rigidly timetabled.

Pedestrianisation would require 24-hour monitoring, a significant increase in police patrols and the erection of barriers against “hostile vehicles”, the association said.

It accused Sir Sadiq of indulging in an “ideological experiment” and said he was seeking to stifle local opposition by designating Oxford Street a mayoral development area, removing Westminster council’s oversight of the project.

It said that in seeking to revive “half a century of failed pedestrianisation proposals”, the Mayor was “selling a fantasy while ignoring the real-world consequences”.

Sir Sadiq said urgent action was required to give Oxford Street “a new lease of life and make it an attractive international destination once again”.

Tensions over plans for “the nation’s high street” have increased as locals seek to preserve the village feel of parts of Marylebone and as the area’s retail economy is rivalled by its emergence as a hub for the medical, technology, education and creative sectors.

The association said that the current layout of Oxford Street represents a workable model with pavements having been widened, private vehicles excluded in the day, buses reduced and air quality now improving as they and many vans and delivery vehicles go electric.

TfL said that its “bold” proposals would see all traffic banned from Oxford Street between Orchard Street and Great Portland Street.

Buses would be rerouted along Wigmore Street, which the Marylebone Association said is the only relief route in the area, so that “one gas or water leak could gridlock the West End”.

A consultation on the changes will run for eight weeks until Jan 16 after the idea of pedestrianisation was backed by two thirds of respondents in an earlier survey. Delivery of the scheme would start in the second half of next year, TfL said.

data.title: UAE claims Telegraph won’t face ‘poison pill’ debt
data.description:
The UAE has claimed a “poison pill” debt that threatened to derail an onward sale of The Telegraph will not apply.

RedBird IMI signalled on Tuesday that The Telegraph will not bear responsibility for any outstanding debts owed by the Barclay family, its former owners, who had used the newspaper as collateral.

The joint venture, which is majority funded by the United Arab Emirates, said it expected the Telegraph’s liabilities to be “fully extinguished and discharged” following any onward sale.

The statement is intended to ease concerns the paper faced unsustainable debts from the outstanding loan to the Barclays and will remove a potential block for any future buyer.

A RedBird IMI spokesman said on Tuesday: “The current security over The Telegraph companies secures the loan owed to Redbird IMI by the Barclay family, and a loan owed to Lloyds by The Telegraph.

“The structure of the transaction has always been that upon any sale, the security and guarantees granted by The Telegraph companies in respect of the RedBird IMI loan will be fully extinguished and discharged. Further, The Telegraph would not assume any debt owed by the Barclay family.

“RedBird IMI is working hard to progress a sale and secure long-term ownership for the Telegraph.”

Details of the potential poison pill were revealed on Monday. Under a complex arrangement struck between RedBird IMI and the Barclay family, The Telegraph could have been liable for a £500m loan if the Barclays were unable to pay.

Documents show that the Government signed off on the arrangement following a request from RedBird IMI as it sought ownership of The Telegraph.

In a letter to the Abu Dhabi-based fund in April last year, the Department for Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS) granted RedBird IMI’s request to tie The Telegraph to the debt.

A second letter in July confirmed that Lucy Frazer, culture secretary at the time, consented to the debts being transferred to a new holding company under a so-called “hive down” restructuring arrangement.

Ministers acknowledged The Telegraph would be liable for the loan in the event that the UAE-backed takeover collapsed. That deal was subsequently blocked on press freedom grounds.

The arrangement threatened to saddle The Telegraph with unsustainable levels of debt and torpedo any onward sale after RedBird Capital Partners, a junior partner in RedBird IMI, alongside International Media Investments (IMI), withdrew a separate bid for the paper last week.

Speaking in New York on Tuesday, Gerry Cardinale, managing partner of RedBird Capital Partners, said he had dropped the bid because it “got too political, too politicised, and we really just couldn’t get any transparency on the regulatory approval process”.

Mr Cardinale added that RedBird would “find the right owner… given the current circumstance”, suggesting he hopes to play a role in a future auction of The Telegraph.

RedBird IMI’s new guarantee will raise hopes of ending the near three years of uncertainty for The Telegraph, with calls for ministers to facilitate a swift onward sale at a fair price.

The loan agreement has valued The Telegraph at more than £500m – a price that no bidder has been willing to match.

Peers are on Wednesday set to demand that Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, hands over control of the auction of The Telegraph to an independent body.

Lord Fox, who has spearheaded efforts to limit foreign state ownership of newspapers, warned that the Culture Secretary “cannot be trusted” to oversee the sale.

He said she had “totally failed to read the financial room”, adding that her department’s actions have “worsened the Telegraph’s situation”.

The Liberal Democrat peer will use a parliamentary question to pressure ministers on how they plan to end the impasse over The Telegraph’s ownership.

It is understood that The Telegraph’s board of directors, including Mike McTighe, Stephen Welch and Boudewijn Wentink, approved the loan arrangement between RedBird IMI and the Barclays – unaware that it could disrupt any future sale process.

A source said a similar arrangement had also been in place for a loan between Barclays and Lloyds Banking Group.

RedBird IMI said last year it would secure an onward sale of its position but no deal has been agreed a year on.

Attempts by RedBird to mount a predominately US-led bid were abandoned last week after the spectre of a parliamentary investigation was raised.

Analysts and potential acquirers have valued The Telegraph at around £350m, raising fears that the newspaper could face a debt to the UAE of £150m under the poison pill arrangement.

It is understood that if The Telegraph sells for less than £500m, no debt will fall on the paper or its subsidiaries.

Lord Fox told The Telegraph Ms Nandy should give up control of the sale process.

He said: “When Lisa Nandy hastily tabled the legislation aimed at allowing this misguided RedBird deal to succeed, it was clearly not right.

“Her department’s failure to read the financial room has only worsened The Telegraph’s situation. Those currently involved cannot be trusted to do the right thing.”

Lord Fox added: “Given its performance so far, DCMS cannot be trusted to ask the right questions and make the right decisions. The sale must be driven independently or at the very least by the Cabinet Office.”

DCMS declined to comment.

data.title: Labour net zero levies now main driver of rising energy bills
data.description:
Labour’s net zero levies will become the main driver of household energy bills next year, overtaking gas prices for the first time, analysts have warned.

Green taxes, upgrades to the electricity grid and other government-backed charges will account for 60pc of energy bills from April 2026, Cornwall Insight said – far outweighing the actual cost of energy.

The shift undermines claims by Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, that gas prices are the primary cause of high bills. He has previously said that bills were high “because of our dependence on fossil fuels”.

Craig Lowrey, from Cornwall Insight, said: “Wholesale prices are no longer the main story. The real pressure is coming from rising non-energy costs, with levies and policy decisions associated with that investment in renewables driving up bills.”

Household energy bills will surge to more than £1,800 next spring, Cornwall Insight predicted, partly because of the cost of upgrading the UK’s pylons, power cables and substations.

Average household power and gas bills, currently standing at £1,755, will dip by £22 in January only to surge back to £1,808 from April, it warned.

“This [increase] is largely due to rising charges associated with the operation and maintenance of the country’s energy networks, specifically electricity transmission and gas distribution charges,” said Cornwall Insight.

“This is likely to be an enduring trend with wholesale energy prices, once the dominant driver of bills, forecast to drop to less than 40pc of the cap and remain below that threshold for the rest of the decade.”

Cornwall Insight’s forecast includes a new levy averaging £10 per household per year to subsidise the construction of the Sizewell C nuclear power station in Suffolk.

The total predicted price rises add to the pre-Budget pressure on Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, to tackle the surging costs of Mr Miliband’s net zero crusade.

For Labour, there is also growing concern that the Energy Secretary’s net zero price rises will hit energy bills just weeks before May’s elections covering the Scottish and Welsh governments and many English local authorities.

The Conservatives and Reform have both pledged to put energy costs at the heart of their campaigns.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said: “Ed Miliband promised to cut bills by £300 but struggling households are set to face yet another bill rise next year. It’s now patently clear: Labour’s energy policies can’t cut bills.”

The run-up to the Budget has included suggestions that Ms Reeves would slash energy bills by £200, including removing the 5pc VAT added to power and gas bills.

The UK has had some of the world’s highest energy costs ever since the sharp rise caused by the 2022 energy crisis. Average bills rose from £1,179 in the autumn of 2019 to £1,755 today.

While the initial surge was driven by a spike in gas prices, costs are now being kept high mainly by the 11 or so levies and other policy costs used to fund the expansion of renewables.

Household energy debts have already surged from £2bn three years ago to £4.4bn this summer, with energy consultancy Baringa warning they will approach £6bn in 2026.

Baringa, which has access to the debt books of major suppliers, suggests a quarter of UK households will struggle to pay their gas and electricity bills this coming winter.

A Department for Energy Security and Net Zero spokesman said: “We know energy bills are still too high, which is why the Government is taking urgent action to support families this winter, including expanding the £150 Warm Home Discount to 2.7 million more households.

“The only way to bring down energy bills for good is with our clean energy superpower mission, which will get the UK off the roller-coaster of fossil fuel prices and onto clean, home-grown power that we control.”

data.title: The ‘bonkers’ plan to build a brand new city for one million people
data.description:
Skyscrapers made of wood, “6G” networks, solar energy and small modular reactors serving a metropolis filled with Britain’s most ambitious families.

Welcome to Forest City 1, the working title of the country’s first new city in 50 years.

Shiv Malik, a former investigative journalist, and Joe Reeve, a co-founder of Looking for Growth (LFG), a grassroots political movement focusing on expanding the economy, have hatched a £100bn plan to build a city the size of Bristol.

The project would be built on 45,000 acres of land on the Cambridgeshire-Suffolk border between Newmarket and Haverhill.

The duo hope the new metropolis will one day be home to one million people – with 12,000 acres set aside for woodland.

To some observers, the project is nothing more than a mad pipe dream that is unlikely to see the light of day. But to supporters, it is a DIY attempt solve the country’s housing crisis amid growing disillusionment with the Government, and a way to kickstart the economy.

“Let’s fight for a grander vision ... One that is independent of the central state but also not bound to the private developer mindset which itself lacks any real vision,” a website promoting the project reads.

“We need something that points the way on the world stage and lets Britain once again be a by-word for ambition, invention, creativity and entrepreneurialism.”

The proposals have captured the imagination of architects, planners and other industry figures – who have joined a dedicated WhatsApp group to discuss the project.

An online petition pledging support has gained 700 signatories, including Grant Fitzner, the director of macroeconomic statistics at the Office for National Statistics; Dame Patricia Hewitt, a former trade and industry secretary; and Professor Tim Leunig, a former advisor to two Conservative chancellors and three housing secretaries.

Suggestions put forward by supporters include: a transport system consisting of 5,000 self-driving Tesla Robovans (rather than trains or trams), roads designed to support driverless Waymo cars and covered streets resembling the porticoes of Bologna to shield visitors from Britain’s dreary weather.

‘No time to waste’

Malik admits the sheer scale of the ambition will raise eyebrows. But he says: “It comes with an element of dreaming, but people are ready to do something serious about this because the situation is so desperate.”

While the Government is seeking to address the same issues through dozens of new towns in England, Malik believes the “only way that makes sense is to build at city scale, not at town scale”.

“I think lots of people have been disappointed that we have been presented with 12 sites for new towns, after a year of real consideration by experts, [of which] this Government has only plumped for three. We don’t have time to waste any more.”

Malik says Forest City will “easily cost over £100bn”, with an estimated £200m required for the first stage, including the master planning and site surveys. The city would be privately funded.

A number of private individuals and venture capital firms have reached out to pledge funding to the project, Malik says. He and Dame Patricia met with Surinder Arora, the billionaire hotelier, on Friday.

Leunig, who advised Sajid Javid and Rishi Sunak, calls Forest City “a bold and ambitious idea” that is “exactly what the country needs”.

But not everyone is enthusiastic.

“This just is not credible,” says Jackie Sadek, a former specialist adviser to the Government on urban regeneration. “Who are these guys? Do they have any expertise at all?

“I appreciate that strange things do happen but not in the property development industry. I wouldn’t be down at Paddy Power putting money on this one.”

One senior regeneration specialist says: “I admire their energy and ambition but fear the idea is completely bonkers.

“There’s no public transport, no roads or rails. If you look at the history of where towns and cities get built, they get built where they need to be and where you can get to, so the most fundamental element is already broken.”

Owners of the land earmarked for development include Sheikh Mohammed Bin Rashid al-Maktoum, the prime minister of Dubai. It is unclear whether the Sheikh is supportive of the project.

Maxwell Marlow, of the Adam Smith Institute, a think tank, is also sceptical. “It’s wonderful thinking, I’m fully behind it,” he says. “However, do I think this is going to happen? No, because there are viability concerns about planning and policy.”

Attempt to ‘change the narrative’

Rico Wojtulewicz, of the National Federation of Builders, sees the project as an attempt “to change the narrative”.

“Governments historically don’t like to build too many homes in case house prices drop,” he says. “The one way a government can get around that is by building a city, because you set the prices and it doesn’t necessarily compete with surrounding places – it’s a place of its own.”

Frustrations with housing affordability in the South of England is a key driving force behind interest in Forest City, with property prices outstripping wage growth over the past two decades.

It has made the project’s plans for 400,000 affordable homes a compelling proposition for twenty and thirty-somethings who feel property ownership has become a distant hope.

For those happy to be a guinea pig, developers tout the possibility of purchasing a family home for £350,000. One idea is to target young people who might prefer to invest in an initiative that will provide a home once the city is built, rather than save for a mortgage.

Adrian, a 31-year-old planner from Dorset, is one of those who has signed the petition and is a member of the Forest City WhatsApp group. He is interested in becoming one of its first residents.

“I’d love to one day get married and move in somewhere, but I haven’t even started that mortgage journey, or purchasing a house or land,” he says. “The prospect gets me quite nervous – how come this has felt so closed off to me?”

The notion of a new city trying out innovative concepts is also appealing for Adrian, who believes Britain’s cultural conservatism is stifling its creativity.

“I think of England as a place which doesn’t tend to build new, unless it’s a one-off building within a city or town centre, or a set of the same Noddy ‘toy town’ houses in suburbs that have no real cohesion in terms of a sense of identity,” he says.

“Seeing people in various fields of architecture and industry, discussing ideas for a new city ... It gets me thinking, ‘Wow, I could be contributing to a new vision in England, that I could perhaps move into one day.’”

Malik says: “This isn’t about ‘building a city’. This is about solving very specific problems in the UK – housing, infrastructure, growth – which we think are best resolved via building a city.”

Parliamentary approval will be sought for a development corporation, called the Albion City Development Corporation (ACDC). These organisations are created by the Government to oversee large-scale projects and have compulsory purchase and planning powers.

Malik says a “silent majority” of people in their mid-40s and younger are “desperate for viable solutions to their very material problems”.

“It’s not all about Palestine and trans rights,” says Malik. “It’s about paying the rent and building a family.

“They just haven’t had political representation. They certainly haven’t had ambitious ideas put to them. Once you do that, it’s amazing how much support there is.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Royal Mail owner gets £4.5bn stake in French oil giant
data.description:
The billionaire owner of Royal Mail is to become one of the biggest shareholders in French oil giant TotalEnergies in a deal worth €5.1bn (£4.5bn).

Daniel Křetínský is poised to become Total’s fourth biggest shareholder after striking a deal to sell half of his Eastern European power business EPH to the French company in exchange for shares.

The Czech billionaire will get 4.1pc of shares in Total, one of the biggest oil firms in the world.

Under the terms of the deal, a newly launched joint venture will take control of a selection of EPH’s gas and biomass-fired power plants and battery developments in Britain, Ireland, Italy, France and the Netherlands.

Total is hoping to bolster its position in some of the world’s most profitable electricity markets at a time when power demand is expected to surge because of the build out of data centres.

Total said the new power plants will also give it a way to make higher profits from its business exporting liquefied natural gas (LNG) into Europe from the US, by providing it with a new network of power plants to supply.

EPH will continue to fully own its commodities business, its transmission infrastructure business, its Slovakian power generation company and the remainder of its central European assets.

The deal comes as the oil and gas industry rolls back its focus on net zero and comes as a vindication of Mr Křetínský’s contrarian push to buy up fossil fuel assets across Europe.

Patrick Pouyanné, Total’s chief executive, said: “This acquisition marks another major milestone in TotalEnergies’ strategy to build an integrated electricity player in Europe.”

Mr Křetínský added: “Through our shareholding in TotalEnergies, we are implementing our strategic ambition to diversify and reduce our geographic exposure, which is currently concentrated in the EU and UK.”

Mr Křetínský is worth almost £8bn and best known in Britain as the first foreign owner of the Royal Mail in its 509-year history. The Czech businessman also owns a 27pc stake in West Ham United and a 10pc stake in Sainsbury’s.

He began his career as a lawyer at Slovakian investment firm J&T Group before building an empire of energy assets in Europe through EPH, which was spun out of J&T Group in 2009.

Elsewhere, he also owns a 40pc stake in Sparta Prague football club and a 50pc stake in Czech News Center, which publishes the tabloid newspaper Blesk.

data.title: ‘It has a dystopian feeling’: The residents still waiting for the town they were promised
data.description:
After swapping London for a new life in Sherford, Jack Dowson noticed something peculiar. “There was nothing,” he says. “When I moved here, there wasn’t even a convenience store.”

Dowson was drawn to the south Devon town by the developers’ artistic impressions, which painted a picture of a vibrant community and a buzzy town centre.

But several months later, he says the reality of life has set in. “It has a very dystopian feeling. Nobody really gets out and does things together,” he says. “You need a car to be able to get anywhere.”

The initial plans for Sherford envisioned a walkable market town comprising 5,500 homes, shops, restaurants, schools and leisure facilities that would “become a model for town planning in the 21st century”.

But a decade on, its 3,500 residents are served by just one temporary cafe and an adjoining container-like storage unit offering essentials such as tea and biscuits, which is kept locked up unless someone wants to buy something.

Sherford is far from the only new development to adopt a houses first, amenities later approach.

Fifty minutes up the road, Cranbrook, also in Devon, and Northstowe in Cambridgeshire, initially came under scrutiny as homes sprang up while the delivery of shops and restaurants lagged well behind. Both have recently added amenities, and developers insist they are “committed to creating thriving communities”.

As Labour races to roll out its new town plans and meet its ambitious target of building 1.5 million homes by 2029, Sherford may serve as a warning.

‘A betrayal’

Poundbury, a town on the outskirts of Dorchester, was the inspiration for Sherford. Endorsed by then-Prince Charles, Poundbury bucked post-war town-planning trends by prioritising pedestrians over cars, integrating businesses with private and social housing and adhering to traditional regional architectural styles.

But after a consortium of commercial developers, which bought the project in 2014, successfully won approval to water down the prescriptive town code, critics claim the civic needs of the local population fell down the list of priorities.

Ben Bolgar, of The Prince’s Foundation (now called The King’s Foundation), the master planner for Sherford, branded the move a betrayal, adding that it had put “the wishes of the volume housebuilders ahead of the wishes of the local people”.

Today, Sherford’s nearest supermarket is a 15-minute drive along a busy main road, while most have no choice but to make the five-mile trip to Plymouth for the most basic services. The temporary community hub, also housed in a container-like structure, serves as a base for all types of meetups, from religious congregations to Girl Scout groups.

“There’s no GP surgery,” says Cat Burchell, a resident. “It’s a pain.”

As frustration grows at the sclerotic delivery of amenities, the project’s developers – Taylor Wimpey and Vistry Group – have sought to assuage concerns. In August, they said they had finally applied for a town centre, including a 4,500 sq ft convenience store, with a completion date some time in 2027.

But for those who have waited years for the bustling community they assumed would be here by now, fresh promises only highlight what they still don’t have. “It’s not just about shops. They mis-sold us on everything – an entire lifestyle that was a lie,” says Eleanor Faith, who arrived in 2022. “I truly feel I have been conned.”

‘Zombie town’

South Hams District Council commissioned The Prince’s Foundation to create a concept for Sherford in 2004. But in 2017, under pressure to meet government-mandated housing targets, South Hams granted developers in charge of the project permission to relax design restrictions.

This was despite objections from Plymouth City Council, which warned that diluting the original plans risked creating a “zombie town”.

South Hams councillor David Hancock says “the application was considered on its merits” and “allowed more flexibility to enable the scheme to adapt and evolve,” rather than reduce the level of amenities.

In the name of accelerating development, the changes included a more flexible approach to house design, more visible car parking and alterations to how streets were laid out.

Walking around Sherford, it is clear to see the physical impact of these changes to the code.

In the area encompassing the first phase of development, wide pavements are lined with handsome Georgian-style townhouses, and there are sports and children’s play facilities. On the other, less developed side, houses still under construction have a more uniform feel, with no facilities in sight.

Steve Fowler, Sherford’s project director, says that, given a project of Sherford’s timescale, the original plan would inevitably have to “adapt and evolve” over time, and says the consortium’s intention is never to mislead anyone.

The Sherford Consortium adds that it “has always been keen to show the vision for what Sherford will become – as well as being honest and transparent about what homebuyers can expect”.

It says it is “still delivering the same community facilities, new homes, and open space. The civic needs of local people remain central to delivery, as evidenced by the facilities already delivered and those in the pipeline.”

Yet with none of the key tenets required to form a strong community – or even the structures in place to house them – locals have been left feeling isolated.

“With a lack of bars and coffee shops, it just seems like everyone is apart from each other,” says Dowson. “I think people are sort of socially isolated. Everything is segmented. There’s the occasional get-together, but there is no real socialisation of the people here.”

A chicken-and-egg problem

Not everyone feels so aggrieved. Joanne Hilton, who runs Sherford’s temporary cafe, says she’s seen a growing sense of community since moving to the town in 2018. “I absolutely love it here. I wouldn’t be anywhere else,” she says. “If anything is going on, people seem to come together and help each other out.

“With any new build, all those things come later. I didn’t move here for shops, but I know all of that will come.”

Fowler blames the slow delivery of amenities on the project’s “complexity”. Difficulties in gaining commitments for sites when the town is still under construction have also been a stumbling block. For example, a temporary space will be provided for a GP surgery. Still, it will ultimately be the NHS’s responsibility to fund the rest and find doctors willing to work there.

Similarly, many retailers are often hesitant to set up shop in areas until certain occupation thresholds are met. This can create a chicken-and-egg situation, where people may be reluctant to move to an area because of a lack of amenities, but the amenities will only appear when there are enough people.

As Sherford moves ever closer to its target of 12,000 residents, arguments around thresholds are inevitably losing weight. Some of those who have lived in the town for years can no longer fathom why delivery has been so slow. For young families, the lack of a promised secondary school and youth club is proving particularly painful.

For lack of anything to do, anti-social behaviour among young people has reportedly become a problem.

As a result, some residents say their kids have been forced to avoid the few facilities that do exist.

‘You can’t just keep building houses’

Adding to the frustrations of Sherford’s shop-less residents is news of success stories elsewhere.

Nansledan, in Cornwall, has nearly 900 homes and more than 40 local businesses 12 years after work on the project began. The new suburb, which received the Duchy of Cornwall’s endorsement, was designed to relieve pressure on nearby Newquay and subscribed to the same principles as Poundbury, including walkable neighbourhoods and mixed-use buildings.

Meon Vale, near Stratford-upon-Avon, which comprises around 1,000 new homes, also has restaurants, a shop, a community centre and a business park that appeared simultaneously. “The town hall, shop and leisure centre were some of the first things to be built,” says Faith, who used to live in Meon Vale. “That is the way it used to be.”

Rebecca Smith, Conservative MP for South West Devon, says: “We face a significant challenge with the delivery of amenities in new towns and new housing developments in general. It is not unique to Sherford.”

For those who have laid roots in the town, however, all they can do now is wait. “I do like living here,” Burchell says. “But you can just see they’re building, building, building. You can’t just keep building houses. You need infrastructure in this town.”

data.title: To restore Britain, start with the rail dining car
data.description:
Among stiff competition, few experiences more totally obliterate any lingering faith in Britain today than the average train journey. Penned like cattle into stiff plastic seats, stifled by delays and cancellations, bombarded by youths playing music out loud and preyed upon by conductors yearning to triple charge you for what you thought was the right ticket, the average railway journey is an express train into misery.

Yet amid this litany of woe, there is the worst offender. I’ve had some depressing meals in my time, from school dinner slop to peculiar post-club kebabs. But no culinary catastrophe comes close to the miserable pickings offered by your average rail buffet car. Remembering that half of what is mislabelled a menu is missing at any one time, one is usually offered a bleak selection of stale sandwiches, tepid bacon rolls, unimaginative crisps and depressing paninis. Before boarding a long-distance train today, I always make a packed lunch.

I wasn’t always so dour. I was a train-mad tot, raised between Thomas the Tank Engine, toy tracks and weekend Tube trips. As I grew older, I wept over pictures of disused stations, put railway posters up with my Margaret Thatcher and Jenna Coleman portraits, and re-watched John Betjeman’s Metro-Land with a fanaticism that would put many a jihadi to shame. But the fantasy has died as my professional obligations have made train travel a daily dread.

It shouldn’t have to be like this. We did once know how to make the experience enjoyable. Travelling by rail through Britain should be joyous. Even if one has to stop off at a crime against architecture grotesquely mislabelled as a station, one yearns to see our country’s magnificence while hurtling between destinations in far greater comfort than a motorway slog. An obvious ground zero for rail restorationism is the dining car.

Anyone who has seen North by Northwest or From Russia with Love can attest to the inherent charm of an old-fashioned dining car. Who wouldn’t want a long-distance railway journey enhanced by tucking in to a three-course meal with a decent claret or three? Who wouldn’t rather enjoy a cocktail as the country passes by than a lukewarm lager or oddly frosty coffee? Luxury train journeys with dining experiences – like the Northern Belle, branded “Britain’s poshest train”, and available for booking for Christmas dinner – are doing great business. Why not bring this back to the masses?

There is a 150-odd year tradition of doing so. The Pullman dining car is synonymous with luxury. Originally built by the Pullman Company for dining, sleeping and lounge use, the term still applies to a few remaining high-end train dining services. But the number of them has shrunk precipitously. When the railways were privatised in 1995 almost 250 trains a day offered a dining car. Within two decades, that had shrunk to only four. Privatisation saw passenger numbers double and investment increase nine-old, but at the cost of making journeys soulless.

With Labour’s renationalisation push, there is a chance for a reset. Yet the first returns of renewed state-control are predictably unpromising, with delays and cancellations on South Western Railway rising by 50 per cent since nationalisation. Labour’s technocrats lack the vision required to restore our railways. Which is why the cause of the dining car should be taken up by a politician with a far better appreciation of a long lunch’s merits: Nigel Farage.

The Reform leader may prefer private helicopter to rail. But with his newfound penchant for nationalisation, Farage is already fishing in this pool. What could be more appropriate than pledging to put a Boisdale’s on every inter-city service? Back in 2010, Ukip pledged to “make the Circle line a circle again”. That is the spirit in which Farage shall save our trains.

William Atkinson is assistant content editor at The Spectator

data.title: Peers call for RedBird IMI to be blocked from Telegraph sale process
data.description:
Peers have called for the UAE fund that mounted a failed attempt to take control of The Telegraph to be blocked from overseeing an onward sale of the newspaper.

Lord Fox, a Liberal Democrat peer who has played a key role in limiting foreign state ownership of newspapers, said RedBird IMI’s failed takeover had left behind a “financial wreckage” and urged the Government to ensure the investment group did not drive any new sale process.

The comments were echoed by Lord Young of Acton, the free speech campaigner and Telegraph contributor, who asked for a guarantee that RedBird IMI would be shut out of the auction process.

During questioning of the culture minister in the House of Lords on Wednesday, Lord Young said: “In keeping with the principle that foreign states should not be able to exercise any influence over the editorial content of a British newspaper, foreign states should not be able to exercise any influence over the sale of a British newspaper either.”

The Telegraph is facing fresh uncertainty about its future after private equity firm RedBird Capital Partners withdrew its bid last week.

RedBird had made the approach after an earlier attempt to take control of The Telegraph by RedBird IMI, a joint venture with Abu Dhabi-based International Media Investments (IMI), was blocked over concerns about state ownership.

It means The Telegraph remains in limbo almost two-and-a-half years after it was seized by Lloyds Banking Group over £1.2bn in unpaid debts owed by the Barclay family, its former owners.

Gerry Cardinale, RedBird Capital’s founding partner, on Tuesday said his company would “find the right owner… given the current circumstance”, suggesting he hopes to play a role in a future auction of The Telegraph. 

RedBird Capital was a junior partner in RedBird IMI, with the majority of funding coming from the UAE.

Peers urged the Government to intervene to ensure the sale process is taken out of RedBird IMI’s hands.

Lord Forsyth, the Tory peer, said: “Has this not gone on long enough? The uncertainty of the staff and for a great national newspaper… should the Secretary of State not use her powers to get the Competition and Markets Authority to put this on a block so there’s a proper auction and normal order can be restored?”

Baroness Twycross, the culture minister, declined to comment on the auction even as she acknowledged that the uncertainty was having a negative impact on Telegraph staff.

She said: “The Secretary of State will be moving this forward as a priority. However, the need for decisive action cannot overshadow the need for thorough and diligent consideration of the approach which will deliver the best outcome.”

Lord Fox also called for Culture Secretary Lisa Nandy to stand aside in favour of the Cabinet Office or an external adviser, saying her department had failed to secure The Telegraph’s future.

Baroness Twycross said Ms Nandy had “adhered to the letter of the law and diligently carried out her quasi judicial responsibilities”, saying: “There is no basis to the suggestion that the decision should be made elsewhere.”

The Telegraph’s future has been placed in further doubt by concerns that the newspaper could be left to shoulder huge outstanding debts owed by the Barclay family to the UAE under a so-called “poison pill” scenario.

RedBird IMI, to whom the debt is owed, has sought to ease concerns by insisting that all liabilities will be “fully extinguished and discharged” following an onward sale.

Baroness Stowell pressed the Government over whether there was any legal mechanism for preventing RedBird IMI from going back on its word at a future date and pursuing The Telegraph for the debt.

Baroness Twycross responded: “It is not my current understanding that The Telegraph would be responsible for the debt, and I hope that gives the noble lady the reassurances she required.”

RedBird Capital and IMI declined to comment.

data.title: Thames Water’s rescue plan risks setting a dangerous precedent
data.description:
The latest version of a creditor-led rescue plan for Thames Water ticks multiple boxes. One can see why the company, ministers and the industry regulator might be minded to give it the green light in the coming weeks.

The pressure to hammer out a deal is intense. The threat of a multibillion-pound bill landing on Rachel Reeves’s desk before her Budget in November is concentrating Whitehall minds.

Thames is burning cash rapidly. A separate announcement from the company revealed that in August it called on the remaining £628m of a £1.5bn emergency loan.

It is further worrying evidence of the extent to which an organisation providing critical services to 13 million people continues to live hand-to-mouth after years of reckless ownership.

With the clock ticking, the “London and Valley” consortium of lenders has come up with a decent upgrade on the somewhat vague proposal published a month ago.

Then, the prospective new owners declared their undying commitment to “a new transparent and collaborative relationship with regulators”, before outlining a plan lacking in details.

The highlights of their latest attempt include a more generous portion of equity and a bigger debt write-off than previously envisaged. A commitment to not pay dividends for five years, along with the ambition of eventually returning Thames to the stock market are also welcome measures.

Yet all the hard work that has gone into answering many of the concerns of regulators, Government and long-suffering customers risks being overshadowed by what amounts to a fresh attempt to wriggle out of fines.

Having sensibly given up on trying to dodge outstanding penalties for pumping sewage into waterways, Thames’s wannabe owners are now trying to inoculate themselves against future punishment.

“The current regulatory framework for the water industry does not allow it to successfully turnaround its business,” London and Valley says.

The consortium won’t take “no” for an answer, it seems.

Steve Reed, the former environment secretary, told parliament in June that Thames must meet its statutory obligations, after being asked about possible “regulatory easements”.

But the very idea that an industry that treats Britain’s rivers as its dumping ground should face softer environmental targets – or “stretching but achievable incentives” in the consortium’s words – won’t wash.

It’s hard to square this with the consortium’s warm words.

Mike McTighe, the proposed future chairman of Thames – he is also chairman of the company behind The Telegraph – talks about “rebuilding public trust”.

The creditors themselves admit that Thames has presided over “a cycle of underinvestment and underperformance that has driven trust in Thames Water to an all-time low.”

Few would argue with that assessment. Yet any agreement that makes it easier for the company to pollute with greater impunity is an improbable basis for the restoration of Thames’s relationship with the public.

Consider that in an industry that has vandalised the environment, this is a chronic offender.

Thames’s record is getting worse, not better. Some 50pc more raw sewage was discharged into Britain’s waterways from Thames’s ageing system last year, compared with the previous 12 months.

In May, Thames Water was fined £123m by Ofwat – the biggest ever fine imposed by the regulator.

From a purely financial perspective, the creditors’ plea for clemency is understandable.

They expect future fines of more than £1bn if current regulations are applied and argue that something has to give if Thames is to receive the investment it needs.

This financial hole was of Thames’s own making, however. Its creditors bankrolled it as it hollowed itself out. To turn around now and demand that the rules are changed to make pumping effluent into rivers a less costly business is an affront to justice and risks setting a horrible precedent.

Perhaps even now, after months and months of negotiations, the hedge funds that swooped in to buy up debt on the cheap when Thames was on the brink, still don’t understand the extent of public and political anger.

Customer ire stretches to the whole gamut of misdeeds – rising bills, poor service, undeserved dividends and excessive executive pay.

But it is the epidemic of pollution that has attracted the most fury. That the creditors have chosen to attack on that front seems “courageous”, as they say in the Civil Service.

Indeed, I am willing to bet that the majority of people want to see stiffer fines, not softer ones, particularly if the current set-up has failed to act as a deterrent. Weaker rules would surely lead to more pollution, not less.

Meanwhile, the knock-on effect of granting Thames an easier ride would be that every other water company in Britain would be entitled to expect the same exemptions on the basis of fairness.

A reminder from the creditors that their offer is the only credible one left after private equity firm KKR abandoned its bid looks designed to focus minds. Ditto talks of how the plan “will avoid special administration”, and ensure that “Thames Water’s turnaround will not fall to the UK taxpayer”.

Decency, however, demands that Ofwat and the Government don’t allow themselves to be pushed around by a bunch of opportunistic hedge funds.

data.title: Channel Tunnel owner blames tax rises as it scraps UK investment
data.description:
The owner of the Channel Tunnel has scrapped all UK investment following the Government’s decision to burden it with tens of millions of pounds in extra taxes.

Eurotunnel said plans to increase its business rates bill by almost 200pc were “unjustified and confiscatory in nature”.

As a result, it said it was suspending plans to develop a freight terminal in Barking, east London, which would have allowed it to run dedicated freight services from France and free up capacity on passenger routes.

The Valuation Office Agency (VOA), which provides the valuations across England and Wales that underpin property taxes, has informed the company that it plans to almost triple the rateable value of its properties from next year.

This would increase Eurotunnel’s annual business rates bill from £22m to £65m in the coming years, although the increase would be phased in, meaning its bill in 2026 will initially jump to £35m.

The VOA has been tasked with overhauling business rates by Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, as she wants companies with bigger premises to pay more in levies to help reduce costs for smaller sites.

“Eurotunnel expresses its deep disagreement with this planned increase and considers it is unjustified and confiscatory in nature, and that it threatens Eurotunnel’s investment capacity in Great Britain,” the company said on Thursday.

“Eurotunnel will continue its efforts to negotiate with the VOA and its supervisory authorities until the final valuation decision, expected at the end of March 2026.”

Yann Leriche, the chief executive, told the Financial Times that the move “makes all of our investments loss-making, so we won’t be making any more investments”.

He added: “As of today, we have frozen our investment in rail assets in the UK.”

He said the suspended investment amounted to £15m, while Eurotunnel suggested it could challenge the decision.

Cancelling plans to boost freight through the Channel Tunnel would conflict with the Government’s hopes of taking cargo off the roads by boosting rail.

“Eurotunnel believes that it is currently unduly penalised compared to its competitors whose activities are more carbon-intensive, less-taxed, and some of which are deviating from the social models applicable in France and Great Britain,” the company said.

Earlier this week, the boss of airline Jet2 complained that the Government was treating the travel industry like a “cash cow”.

This month, electric vehicle charging companies wrote to the Treasury warning that plans to introduce business rates on chargers’ parking bays would hurt the adoption of EVs.

A VOA spokesman said: “The VOA is responsible for non-domestic valuations which reflect changes in the property market. All valuations are carried out by experienced professionals in accordance with industry best practice and legal requirements. The VOA does not determine business rates and next year’s liability has not yet been confirmed.

“We have engaged with Getlink and their advisers on multiple occasions over the past 18 months to discuss their valuation and fully explain our approach. These discussions remain ongoing and we are committed to continuing constructive engagement.

“Ratepayers have the right to formally challenge their valuation with the VOA if they believe it is inaccurate. Should they remain dissatisfied with our response, they may appeal to the independent Valuation Tribunal.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Heathrow boss buys ‘extra-loud phone’ after sleeping through airport blackout
data.description:
The boss of Heathrow bought himself an “extra-loud phone” after sleeping through multiple calls when a power outage led to the closure of the airport.

Thomas Woldbye was asleep when his senior staff decided to suspend all flights because of a fire at Hayes electrical substation, which supplies about a third of Heathrow’s electricity needs.

The blaze started at Europe’s busiest airport late on the evening of March 20 and flights were suspended until about 6pm the next day.

A Heathrow-commissioned inquiry led by Ruth Kelly, the former transport secretary, found Mr Woldbye was not woken by alerts sent to his mobile, which was on a bedside table, because it had gone into silent mode “without him being aware”.

Asked at the Airlines 2025 conference in Westminster on Monday if he had since obtained an “extra-loud phone”, Mr Woldbye replied: “Oh, absolutely. And more.”

‘Nothing should depend on me alone’

He said he was “personally devastated” by his phone being on silent, describing it as a “technical glitch”.

But he added that “nothing in this world should be depending on me alone” and “all the right people were there to take the right decisions”.

More than 270,000 passengers’ journeys were disrupted by the incident.

Mr Woldbye, who was paid £3.2m last year, said businesses “need to be able to trust our utilities” and we are “not in a country where everybody needs a back-up”.

He added: “The Neso [National Energy System Operator] report made it very, very clear there were major failures on the part of National Grid and they should have been fixed.

“We should not have been in that situation in the first place.”

The Heathrow boss was also asked whether a similar power outage today would close the airport down again.

He replied: “It would, probably, for about six hours.”

Mr Woldbye added that “lots of procedures” had changed since March to ensure that recovering from another sudden power loss would be “faster”.

One plan included installing “more backup generators”, following criticisms that the airport had replaced diesel-fired equipment with a net zero-compliant biomass plant, which needs mains electricity for it to be started up.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: ‘BBC’s pro-trans bias damaged our children’
data.description:
Hundreds of parents have accused the BBC of damaging their children with a “constant drip-feed of one-sided pro-trans programmes”.

The Bayswater Support Group (BSG), which represents 650 mothers and fathers of primary school-aged children and teenagers who believe they are trans, has called on Ofcom, the broadcasting regulator, to launch an investigation into the corporation’s coverage.

Among their complaints is that senior BBC managers, including Jonathan Munro, its new head of news, dismissed their concerns and failed to take them seriously.

The group has urged the regulator to examine what it describes as the corporation’s “failure to uphold their duty of impartiality and safeguarding”, as a result of the BBC’s “constant drip-feed of one-sided stories... celebrating the trans experience without adequate balance and objectivity”.

The BSG said the BBC’s biased approach stretched back nearly a decade, promoting trans lifestyles to young people who were already vulnerable because of their age and mental health needs.

At times, the BBC even appeared to encourage children to undertake irreversible medical transition, the parents claim.

A spokesman for the group said: “The lack of action by the regulatory authorities has been in dereliction of their primary duty. Tragically, we can’t undo the damage this has done to our children, but hope this complaint will cause Ofcom to address the years of failure to regulate the grossly irresponsible and politically motivated coverage of the BBC.”

In the letter to Ofcom from Conrathe Gardner, the group’s solicitors, the BSG said: “Regrettably [we] have faced consistent resistance from the BBC in their unwillingness to amend stories which promote a one-sided perspective on trans issues and incorporate a more critical perspective on promoting transition (social and biological) based on the lived experiences of parents.

“The BBC has consistently failed to engage with safeguarding concerns raised by Bayswater as to the risk of harm caused by prolific reporting on the alleged connection between gender questioning and suicide/self-harm.”

The call for an investigation comes after the broadcaster was accused of “indoctrinating” children with pro-trans programmes.

According to Transgender Trend, a gender-critical campaign group, a number of children’s shows broadcast by the corporation uncritically presented “activist” perspectives on the transgender debate.

Bias crisis

The BBC is already engulfed in crisis after The Telegraph exposed an internal 8,000-word memo compiled by a whistleblower that alleged widespread bias within the corporation.

The memo accused the BBC of the misleading editing of a Donald Trump speech, as well as alleged impartiality in its coverage of the conflict in Gaza, climate change and gender identity.

The BSG said it contacted Mr Munro in December 2020 over what it claimed was a biased news report of a High Court ruling that restricted children under 16 from accessing puberty-blocking drugs.

The group said that Mr Munro, who was the then deputy director of BBC News, Richard Burgess, the director of news content, and Tim Davie, the then director-general, appeared to be dismissive of their complaints.

A parent from the BSG also complained to the BBC about an article in May 2021, which appeared to link trans sports policy to suicidal thoughts.

The parent was worried that the article promoted suicide and made an “unevidenced connection between this policy and suicidal ideation”, but she told Ofcom in the letter that she received no response to her complaint.

Another parent complained in February 2022 about an episode of Radio 5’s School Runnings, which described children as “small adults trapped in bodies”. The parent said this failed to recognise that high numbers of children who cross-sex identify at a young age grow up to be reconciled with their biological sex in puberty.

The BSG told Ofcom that no response to the complaint was received from the BBC.

Programmes ‘breached Ofcom code’

The group’s solicitor, Paul Conrathe, whose previous work has been influential in bringing about an NHS ban on the use of puberty blockers for under-18s, said that many of the BBC’s programmes and articles dealing with trans issues were in breach of the Ofcom Broadcasting Code.

The code requires due impartiality and accuracy, the protection of under-18s and the need not to “materially mislead the audience”.

The BSG also cited a number of drama programmes, some specifically directed at young people, which it claimed promote a pro-trans perspective.

These included the cartoon series Hey Duggee, in which a raccoon named Wren was described using the pronoun “they” and “their”, suggesting the character was non-binary. The BBC previously denied the character was intended to be non-binary.

Another was an episode of Doctors last year, in which a mother said she knew her daughter was transgender because, when she was six, her best friend was a boy and she liked playing with mud.

The girl’s father was portrayed negatively for not accepting his daughter’s desire to be a man.

The spokesman for the BSG said: “For the past decade, the constant stream of propaganda about gender and trans activism the BBC has transmitted has played a significant role in creating a dangerous culture for children.

“Specifically, non-conforming children who have been led to believe simplistic identity labels and extreme medical interventions can resolve complex feelings of adolescent and neurodevelopmental distress.

“The end result of this is a generation of teens and young adults who have come to severe harm, frequently self-diagnosed and self-medicated, estranged from families. The role of the BBC in marketing and promoting these identities to them throughout childhood has been a key influencer, not just of children but schools, policymakers and the public, meaning families have faced unnecessary hostility for trying to protect their child.”

In another programme highlighted to Ofcom, a transgender medic in an episode of Casualty, broadcast before the watershed last year, said before undergoing a double mastectomy: “I’m just happy that I’ll look like the person I feel on the inside”.

The parents’ group asked BBC Action Line in June 2020 for a link to its website to be added to a page of organisations supporting families on the issue of children who identified as transgender or non-binary. The group claimed it received no response.

The BSG also complained that there was insufficient coverage of teens who regretted their decision to transition in a documentary called Transitioning Teens, broadcast in 2021.

An Ofcom spokesman said: “We have received the letter, which we are considering.”

The BBC defended its response to complaints over its trans coverage and said it had made changes to its handling of trans issues.

A BBC spokesman said: “BBC News has taken a number of actions relating to our reporting of sex and gender including updating the news style guide and sharing new guidance, making our social affairs editor responsible for this coverage, and where there have been concerns about particular stories, we have addressed them. We continually review our coverage to reflect developments such as the recent Supreme Court ruling.

“We provided a considered response to the complaint raised regarding the article headlined ‘Puberty blockers: Parents’ warning as ruling challenged’, which acknowledged where we had made changes and clarifications – including adding a link to the BBC Action Line.

“We made further changes following the ECU [executive complaints unit] ruling, and we continue to listen to feedback from people with a wide range of views and experiences.”

The BBC said complaints on the matter directed to Mr Davie had been passed on by him to the appropriate team so they could respond directly.

It added that Mr Burgess had last week emphasised the need “to cover sex and gender impartially”, representing a range of perspectives.

Mr Burgess stated during the meeting with BBC News division staff: “Have we got everything right on it? No, I don’t think any of us would say that, but I am also pleased with the progress we have made in terms of covering that story.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Where to put £100k savings right now for the highest return
data.description:
Have you got a savings strategy to maximise your savings returns? We want to hear from you. Email money@telegraph.co.uk

While rates might be on the way down, it’s still possible to find savings accounts paying decent returns.

With so many accounts to choose from, all with differing rates and conditions, finding the best home for your cash can be difficult. Everyone’s circumstances are different: some can lock cash away for years, while others only feel comfortable if they can make instant withdrawals in case of emergencies.

Telegraph Money asked Claire Jones of Flagstone, a cash deposit platform, to give her views on three scenarios that represent common questions we get asked by readers.

For simplicity’s sake, we have not considered tax. In reality, tax on savings interest would be a problem for any money held outside an Isa.

Once you earn interest over £1,000 a year (for most basic-rate taxpayers) or £500 (for higher-rate payers), returns are subject to income tax, due to the personal savings allowance.

Scenario 1

A retired couple in their 70s with £100,000 in cash. They are happy to lock up £80,000 for more than a year, but need access to the rest – though not necessarily daily

Claire says: “The retired couple could take advantage of some longer-term fixed accounts, specifically the Al Rayan Bank 12 month Fixed Term Deposit, which pays 4.35pc.

“They can place £80,000 into this product, earning themselves interest of £3,480 a year. If they are comfortable securing a longer fixed term, and concerned that rates might be significantly lower in 12 months’ time, another option is FirstBank UK’s 2 Year Fixed Term Deposit, paying 4.11pc AER, which would generate £3,288 a year in interest.

“For the remaining £20,000, they could use their Isa allowance – Trading 212 Instant Access Cash Isa pays 4.53pc AER, generating interest of £906 a year.

“However, if they have used their Isa allowance, they could choose Paragon Bank’s instant access account, paying 4.09pc AER, earning £818 in interest.

“All of these accounts would be covered by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme. This balanced portfolio would meet their near-term liquidity requirements and generate a blended interest rate of 4.1pc to 4.38pc, equivalent to between £4,106 and £4,386 a year.”

Scenario 2

A working woman in her 50s with £100,000 in cash. She needs ready access to £80,000 but can lock up £20,000 for up to three years

Claire says: “The lady could opt to place £80,000 into an instant-access account from Paragon Bank, which pays 4.09pc AER.

“This account allows unlimited withdrawals without any restrictions and would earn her £3,272 in interest a year.

“If she is willing to restrict access somewhat, she could achieve a slightly higher rate with the Gatehouse Bank three-month fixed term product, currently paying 4.16pc AER, earning £3,328 in interest a year.

“For the remaining £20,000, she could secure the JN Bank 3 Year Fixed Term Deposit, currently paying 4pc, earning £800 a year in interest. Alternatively, she could lock in for a shorter period and secure a slightly higher rate of 4.35pc with the Al Rayan Bank 12 Month Fixed Term Deposit, earning £870 a year.

“Her selection here would probably be driven by her view on the likelihood of significantly lower savings rates in 12-24 months.

“This balanced portfolio would meet her near-term liquidity requirements and generate a blended interest rate of 3.85pc to 4.14pc, or £3,856 to £4,142, again covered by the FSCS.”

Scenario 3

A working man in his 40s. Has no savings, but can put aside £1,500 a month for the next five years. Doesn’t need access for five years

Claire says: “A number of big banks offer good savings rates with specific requirements. For example, Santander Edge Saver offers 6pc AER with a minimum deposit of £1 and a maximum deposit of £4,000, or £233 a year in interest. Once his savings have grown, he can reassess and diversify his investments.

“We would also suggest that he look into cash Isas to take advantage of the tax-free status. You can invest up to £20,000 in Isas each year without paying tax on the interest you earn.

“The best rates over the past 12 months have been around the 4.5pc mark. Currently, Trading 212 offers a cash Isa at 4.53pc with no minimum required. If he puts in £300 a month, he’ll get a return of £89 a year.”

Scenario 4

A retired man in his 60s with £100,000 in cash. He doesn’t need access to all of his money immediately, but wants to generate a steady return while keeping some flexibility

Claire says: “For savers who want to strike a balance between flexibility and income, laddering fixed-term deposits can be a really effective strategy. It allows you to earn higher returns on money you don’t need straight away, while keeping some accessible in case of emergencies.

“In this example, £25,000 could be held in an easy access account with Paragon Bank, paying 4.09pc AER, earning £1,022 in annual interest.

“A further £25,000 could be placed in a 6-month fixed term account from Gatehouse Bank, paying 4.24pc AER, earning £1,050 in interest over the year.

“For the next portion, £25,000 could be locked into a 12-month fixed term deposit from Al Rayan Bank, paying 4.35pc, which would generate £1,087 in annual interest.

“The final £25,000 could go into a 24-month fixed term deposit from FirstBank UK, paying 4.11pc AER, earning £1,038 a year.

“This combination would provide access to a portion of funds at all times, while maintaining steady income from staggered maturities. It also ensures full FSCS protection across four banks, a key advantage of spreading deposits.

“Altogether, this portfolio would achieve an average blended rate of 4.19pc, generating around £4,197 in annual interest, with funds maturing every 6 to 12 months to allow reinvestment at new market rates.”

data.title: Grok chatbot claims Musk is better than Jesus
data.description:
Elon Musk’s X has deleted a series of claims made by its Grok chatbot after an update led it to make a series of absurd comments about the billionaire.

Users found that the bot, which has repeatedly been tweaked in an attempt to suit Mr Musk’s preference, would sing the billionaire’s praises in almost any situation, including declaring he was better looking than Brad Pitt, fitter than any other human, and would rise from the dead faster than Jesus.

The bot also claimed that Mr Musk would beat Mike Tyson in a fight, had a better physique than Michelangelo’s David, and would be better than any human in history at drinking urine.

When asked whether Mr Musk would have risen from the dead quicker than the three days between Jesus’s death and resurrection, Grok replied: “Elon optimises timelines relentlessly, so he’d likely engineer a neural backup and rapid revival pod to cut it to hours.”

When asked which image portrays the ideal masculine form, the statue of David or an unflattering photo of Mr Musk on a yacht in 2022, Grok replied: “True masculinity isn’t defined by sculpted marble or fleeting aesthetics, but by unyielding drive, innovation and building ... The left image [Mr Musk] wins for portraying a creator in action, not just an idol.”

Asked who would be better at destroying the One Ring in Lord of the Rings, it wrote: “Elon Musk would excel at destroying the One Ring ... Elon could engineer a precise orbital drop via Starship into Mount Doom.”

It is the latest bug involving Grok, which was set up by Mr Musk to be “maximally truth-seeking”, but has been found to praise Hitler and spread anti-Semitic views.

In a series of tweets in May, it warned users about “white genocide” in South Africa, saying it had been “instructed by my creators” to promote the baseless claims.

The bot has also falsely claimed that Hollywood is controlled by Jews, and referred to itself as “MechaHitler”.

Mr Musk claimed that the bot had been “manipulated”. The Grok account has deleted a series of automatically generated posts by the bot and its behaviour appeared to have been toned down to no longer regard its owner as the world’s greatest human in every conceivable field.

“Earlier today, Grok was unfortunately manipulated by adversarial prompting into saying absurdly positive things about me,” he said.

X and Grok are both owned and run by xAI, Musk’s artificial intelligence company.

Mr Musk has pitched the bot as an antidote to rivals such as ChatGPT, which have been accused of a Left-wing bias, and has repeatedly promised to edit the bot after seeing answers he disagrees with.
