data.title: ‘Uninvestable’ UK takes 30 years to do a nine-month project, says billionaire
data.description:
Britain is increasingly “uninvestable” because straightforward projects can take decades to complete, a billionaire heir of a diamond mining dynasty has said.

Jonathan Oppenheimer, a South African businessman and veteran of De Beers and Anglo American, both of which his family built, said extraordinarily slow decision-making and planning rules meant it is not worth putting money into the UK.

He cited as evidence the dualling of the A66, a crucial route between England’s east and west coasts, running from Teesside to Workington in Cumbria.

“So long as the UK takes 30 years to do a nine-month project, it’s uninvestable,” Mr Oppenheimer told the Bloomberg Africa Business Summit.

Plans to turn the stretch from Penrith to Scotch Corner into a dual carriageway were launched in 2002, but only partially completed. Further proposals made in 2016 were approved in 2024.

Mr Oppenheimer has deep family and personal links to Britain. He was educated at Harrow School in London and at the University of Oxford.

Oppenheimer Partners, his investment group, has major business interests across sub-Saharan Africa, yet it is Britain which he has singled out as suffering from cumbersome decision making and project management issues.

The businessman is a scion of the storied Anglo-South African Oppenheimer family, which has long links with major British companies including London-headquartered, FTSE 100 mining giant Anglo American and De Beers, the former diamond monopoly.

Mr Oppenheimer’s comments will be particularly galling for the Government as it seeks to attract more international investment into the UK.

Labour is struggling to convince foreign investors to back infrastructure projects in Britain, despite hopes that extra spending by Whitehall will inspire confidence and “crowd in” private sector investment in long-term schemes.

The Government sought to advertise Britain’s potential as a home for international money by hosting an investment summit shortly after taking office last year.

However, ministers have struggled to attract wealth, with rising taxes putting off entrepreneurs and other big spenders whose presence might be a useful boost for the economy.

The abolition of the non-dom status and the threat of a wealth tax are among policies driving moneyed investors to seek homes in locations including Dubai and Milan instead of London.

Complaints over Britain’s infrastructure projects extend far beyond the A66.

Earlier this year China opened the world’s highest bridge, which spans the Huajiang Canyon and soars to twice the height of London’s Shard skyscraper.

It was completed in just three-and-a-half years – the same length of time it took to build a flyover on the M67 in Denton, on the outskirts of Manchester.

data.title: Hotel tycoon attacks Heathrow boss over runway plans
data.description:
Hotel tycoon Surinder Arora has accused Heathrow bosses of “propagating myths” about his bid to build a third runway at Europe’s busiest airport.

Mr Arora said Heathrow had waged a campaign to undermine his proposals for a shorter, cheaper strip than the one proposed by the airport itself. He called on Thomas Woldbye, the airport’s chief executive, to debate the rival plans.

The tycoon said Heathrow falsely claimed his scheme would mean the demolition of “a crazy number of houses” and require a motorway spur from the M4 to the airport to be lowered beneath the proposed runway, interfering with railway lines.

Mr Arora shot back and claimed the airport had failed to explain how its own longer runway would span the M25 without the rebuilding of many more road links that it had admitted to.

The tussle over the rival runway bids has turned personal days before ministers are due to decide which of the two to take forward as part of Labour’s plan to encourage long-term growth through big-ticket infrastructure projects.

Heathrow has proposed a 3,500-metre strip to the north west of the current runways. While it would lift capacity to 150 million passengers a year, it would cost £49bn to deliver and involve redirecting the M25 under the new runway.

Meanwhile, Mr Arora, who owns hotels around Heathrow, has proposed building a 2,800-metre runway with a price tag of no more than £25bn that would avoid the M25 and, he claims, accommodate almost as many flights as the airport’s own blueprint.

He said: “Our dear friends at Heathrow will always try to propagate myths and doubts about us. But when it comes to their own scheme, it’s smoke and mirrors.

“If Thomas Woldbye wants to stand up in public and have a debate, I’m more than happy to do that, because we’ve got nothing to hide.

“People said to me at the start, are you sure you want to do this? It’s David against Goliath. Heathrow are a big machine, they’ve got big shareholders and they’re going to try to blow you away. And that’s what they’ve been trying to do.”

The Department for Transport (DfT) is expected to announce its preferred option for the third runway by the end of the month. It is understood that it may do so before the Budget in order to give Chancellor Rachel Reeves the opportunity to champion the project.

As a decision looms, Heathrow revealed the chief executives of 13 suppliers and terminal retailers had written to Heidi Alexander, the transport secretary, expressing “full support” for its plans. The companies included retailers Burberry and Fortnum & Mason and builder Costain.

Mr Arora said Heathrow was “clutching onto straws” after seeing support for its runway plan, part of a £49.1bn expansion scheme, ebbing away.

Sean Doyle, the chief executive of British Airways, said last week his company, the biggest airline at Heathrow, was open to a shorter runway and said if the M25 could be avoided, it should be.

Mr Arora said: “They never thought they would be in this position, having to justify their existence. But the train has left the station. All their behaviour over the last decades and their abuse of their monopoly and lack of value for money for customers and airlines, all that is coming out.

“People are beginning to realise that the regulatory system is broken and that the track record of these guys is so bad. Everybody is fed up and saying enough is enough.

“If I was knocking on your door with that track record and saying I am the most expensive builder and you’re going to get crap service, because I’m not even in the top 20 in the world, wouldn’t you slam the door in my face?”

Carlton Brown, Arora Group’s finance chief, said he expected the decision on the runway to turn on whether or not the Government trusts Heathrow to build a strip over the M25.

He said: “There is only one real question: can you avoid the M25 or not? That is the problem the DfT now faces, because we have shown definitively you can do it.”

A Heathrow spokesman said its plans been closely scrutinised for a decade and previously won the support of Parliament, “so it’s hardly smoke and mirrors.”

He said the notion that Heathrow was delivering a poor service passenger “is not reflective of reality,” with the hub the most punctual in Europe and ranked 22nd out of more than 500 worldwide according to Skytrax passenger surveys.

data.title: Bidding war rises over weight loss drug that could prevent ‘Ozempic face’
data.description:
Two of the world’s largest pharmaceutical companies are locked in a bidding war for the maker of a weight-loss drug that could help patients avoid succumbing to “Ozempic face”.

Danish giant Novo Nordisk has launched an attempt to gatecrash Pfizer’s $7.3bn (£5.6bn) takeover of US biotech Metsera by tabling an offer of up to $9bn.

Metsera, which had already agreed to the Pfizer deal, described the approach from Novo Nordisk as “superior”.

However, Pfizer hit back by branding the offer as “reckless”. The US pharmaceutical giant now has four working days to salvage a deal.

It comes as drug giants scramble to secure the newest and most innovative weight-loss treatments to meet soaring demand.

Metsera is developing treatments based on the hormone amylin, which researchers have suggested could stop people from losing muscle during rapid weight loss.

Currently, Eli Lilly’s Mounjaro and Novo Nordisk’s Wegovy help people to shed pounds, but also lead them to lose muscle as well as fat.

The same goes for Novo’s Ozempic, which is a diabetes drug used off-label for weight loss.

‘Ozempic face’

As well as being praised for the weight-loss credentials, the drugs have also been blamed for the rise of “Ozempic face”. This is where people receiving treatment start to appear gaunt as they lose muscle mass in their cheeks.

However, researchers have suggested that amylin could help people to maintain muscle even as they lose weight, as the hormone appears to play a role in maintaining body composition.

Dimitris Papamargaritis, an associate professor at the University of Leicester, earlier this year said: “Early lab studies suggest that adding amylin could help people lose more fat while sparing muscle, though this still needs confirmation in humans.”

Pharmaceutical companies have become increasingly aware of the issue as the drugs grow in popularity.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the AstraZeneca chief executive, last year said weight-loss medicines needed to be more targeted towards fat to help people avoid losing too much muscle in the long term.

He said: “Today you lose weight but you lose fat and you lose muscle. The problem with many of [the treatments] is when you stop, you regain weight, because obesity is a chronic disease.

“Most people, as soon as they stop taking the medicines, they regain fat, but not so much the muscle that they have lost unless, of course, they go to the gym.”

AstraZeneca is developing its own weight-loss treatments, with the aim of leapfrogging rivals with new, potentially cheaper, obesity drugs. In 2023, it bought a Chinese biotech which was working on a weight-loss pill.

Metsera is also developing pill-based weight-loss drugs, as well as longer-lasting treatments. It is testing one injection that people would only have to take once a month, compared to current weight-loss jabs that require once-a-week injections.

Novo Nordisk made the offer for the US biotech as it battles a slump in performance, having recently announced plans to cut 9,000 jobs.

data.title: Tories pledge to ban diversity targets for City firms
data.description:
Diversity targets imposed on City boardrooms would be axed under a new policy unveiled by the Conservatives to slash red tape.

Sir Mel Stride, the shadow chancellor, announced proposals on Thursday to ban Britain’s main financial watchdogs from introducing new rules on diversity and repeal existing regulations imposed on major high street banks and insurers.

Under the Tory plans, the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) and Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) would be forced to focus strictly on financial issues when making new rules by requiring the regulators to obtain ministerial approval for any “non-financial” rules.

Sir Mel said the party would also sweep away any rules for banks and insurers which do not relate to financial stability, competition, consumer protection or growth, it it wins power at the next election.

The move is designed to free City firms from having to comply with diversity and inclusion rules, which have increased compliance costs for businesses.

These include FCA diversity targets for banks and investment managers to have 40pc of board seats assigned to women and at least one seat occupied by a person from an ethnic minority background.

“If we want to compete in the world, British business must be freed from unnecessary costs and red tape,” Sir Mel said. “For too long, governments have imposed burdens on firms around issues such as climate change and diversity, while encouraging the independent regulators to do the same.”

The proposals come amid a wider shake-up at the regulators following pressure from Rachel Reeves to cut red tape.

Earlier this year, the Chancellor urged Nikhil Rathi, the FCA chief, and Sam Woods, the PRA boss, to come up with ideas to boost UK growth after claiming City regulations were acting like a “boot on the neck” of business.

The move prompted a major rethink from regulators, with Mr Rathi pledging to roll back some of the more onerous rules. However, he has warned that greater risk-taking will lead to a greater chance of consumer harm in future.

It came after the FCA and the PRA scrapped plans to introduce new diversity and inclusion reporting rules for City firms over concerns about imposing extra costs on banks.

The watchdogs had faced a backlash over the plans from City executives, before abandoning the proposals in March following Donald Trump’s crackdown on diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) initiatives in the US.

The Tory proposals are part of a blitz by the party on business regulations, which included an announcement last week to scrap mandatory reporting of sustainability and climate change issues.

Sir Mel added: “I want the UK to have the most dynamic financial services industry in the world. That means companies should be allowed to focus on delivering value for customers and shareholders, not on targets, quotas and reporting requirements dictated by government.”

The FCA and PRA were contacted for comment.

data.title: Why Jane wouldn’t mind Austen Country being concreted over
data.description:
It is a truth universally acknowledged, that any bucolic English village in possession of a good few free fields, must be in want of a housing development. Not quite, say the villagers of east Hampshire, who are arguing that a proposed development of 12,000 new homes between Farnham and Alresford will leave the historic countryside which inspired Jane Austen “concreted over”. “Save Jane Austen Country”, the cry goes.

There’s every reason to be sympathetic to those opposed to the plans, which are still in the early development stage known as a “land availability assessment”. If the building did go ahead, it would likely decimate wildlife in Hampshire’s chalk streams and destroy local farmland, and there has been no indication it would be supported by a corresponding expansion of local services like GPs and schools.

But when it comes to the claims that this development would see “Austen Country” destroyed, I come over as disagreeable as Lady Catherine de Bourgh. Bar the scenes of Bath’s creamy crescents and the drama of Emma’s excursion to Box Hill in Surrey, is Jane Austen really a landscape writer?

Before anyone cites Colin Firth’s infamous emergence from the lake, it’s worth remembering that the sultry scene was a decidedly Nineties addition to the television adaptation of Pride and Prejudice, rather than one dreamt up by Miss Austen herself. And, besides, you would have a hard time proving it is Hampshire’s rugged beauty that any viewer’s mind is focused on in that exact moment.

Jane Austen is a writer focused on interior scenes: awkward tea parties, tense balls and the difficult politics of a country house stay. Unlike the wuthering moors of the Brontës’ novels, the emotional heart of Austen’s novels is always in the dim light of a drawing room or rickety carriage, or contained within the handwritten lines of a letter.

Even if her heroines do get themselves into scrapes in their surrounding landscapes – a sprained ankle and rain-induced fever jolt the plot along in Sense and Sensibility – you would be hard-pressed to argue that Austen is a nature novelist, more inspired by the sparkling dew on the grass than by glint in the eye of a locally-stationed young army officer.

The “Save Austen Country” campaign relies on seeing “Austen Country” as some perfect, historical Eden; an apolitical arcadia, which can’t risk being touched or sullied by modern life. It’s a romantic fallacy, but one which wouldn’t have held up even in Austen’s time. Her landscapes hum to the background of Regency-era politics, with dashing soldiers in military encampments and, in the case of Mansfield Park, a suggestion that some of splendid wealth on display is derived from colonial trade. Indeed, Austen sends up one of her characters – the dreamy, novel-obsessed Catherine Morland in Northanger Abbey – for wishing for a landscape of gothic “ancient oaks”, and being disappointed by the ease of “modern taste” she finds instead.

Austen may well have been inspired by the Hampshire countryside – as those in the campaign point out, it is certainly a beautiful place to live and write – but there is something disingenuous about using her work to oppose new houses. This was a woman who came from a middle-class family; a family who were continually squeezed for money. Perhaps the 21st-century Jane Austen will write from Hampshire too, but from a new build instead.

data.title: Mobile data use hits record high as Gen Z shuns broadband
data.description:
Britons are burning through mobile data at record rates amid growing signs that Gen Z is shunning conventional broadband.

Mobile data use in the UK topped 1.2 billion gigabytes each month this year, up by almost a fifth from 2024 and a new record high, according to figures from Ofcom.

That is the equivalent of streaming 400 million football matches in HD, watching 315 billion TikTok posts or sending 1.3 quadrillion text-only WhatsApp messages.

While 4G still accounts for the majority of mobile data traffic, 5G use rose by more than half this year.

The figures highlight how the dominance of smartphones has left data-hungry Britons more reliant on mobile internet than on fixed broadband alternatives.

The rollout of full-fibre broadband has continued at pace, with 78pc of UK homes now able to access the service, up from 69pc last year.

However, just 42pc of households with access to full-fibre broadband have taken it up. While that is up from 35pc last year, it means millions of people are still shunning faster, more reliable connections.

Separate research by analysts at New Street Research forecasts the number of broadband subscribers across the UK will fall by 250,000 this year – the first decline in history.

BT and other broadband providers are facing growing competition from satellite alternatives such as Elon Musk’s Starlink, which promises coverage in even the most remote rural areas via a network of satellites in low earth orbit.

Starlink topped 100,000 customers in the UK for the first time this year, according to the Ofcom report.

A growing number of customers are also opting for Fixed Wireless Access (FWA), a form of broadband that uses mobile signals rather than fixed infrastructure. This is expected to be a significant growth target for VodafoneThree following the £15bn merger of the two mobile networks.

Meanwhile, some Britons are shunning broadband altogether and simply tethering laptops or TVs to their smartphones. This is thought to be particularly prevalent among Gen Z students living in short-term accommodation.

Initiatives to keep up with demand

The growing dominance of mobile internet has left network operators racing to keep up with demand.

Outdoor 5G coverage is available across 97pc of the UK from at least one provider – up from 95pc last year.

But research shows Britain ranks lowest for 5G download speeds in the G7, and MPs have complained that patchy coverage is harming productivity.

BT-owned EE, O2 and VodafoneThree are all deploying so-called “standalone 5G” – an upgraded form of current 5G networks – in a bid to provide faster and more reliable signals.

The Ofcom data showed that 83pc of the UK now has access to standalone 5G from at least one provider.

Natalie Black, Ofcom’s head of connectivity, said: “The UK’s demand for data continues to grow as we live increasingly connected lives.

“For years, operators have been delivering 5G services while using old 4G networks to do most of the legwork. But now, the race to deliver the UK’s full 5G future is on.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Cutting energy bills won’t solve Reeves’s inflation headache
data.description:
UK inflation remains stubbornly high, and the Government’s own policies are largely to blame. Rachel Reeves will therefore be even keener to announce some measures to cut household bills in next week’s Budget. However, she is unlikely to do much to soften the blow from another round of painful tax increases.

At first sight, the latest news on inflation was good: the headline rate fell to 3.6pc in October, from 3.8pc in September. But this decline, which was already widely expected, does not mean that prices are down or that the cost of living crisis is easing.

In fact, the Consumer Price Index (CPI) rose by another 0.4pc in October compared to September. The 3.6pc annual inflation rate also still means that prices were 3.6pc higher than in the same month of 2024.

Moreover, the fall in headline inflation can be entirely explained by the favourable base effects in energy prices. In short, gas and electricity bills rose by less this October than they did in the same month last year. But they still increased by another 2pc from their already high levels.

The quarterly review of the Ofgem cap added another £35 to the typical annual bill, pushing it even further away from the mythical £300 savings promised by Ed Miliband.

Most other components of inflation were little changed. But food price inflation jumped again, from 4.5pc to 4.9pc. This is especially worrying because poorer households tend to spend a larger proportion of their budgets on food and because inflation expectations are particularly sensitive to the prices of items bought more frequently.

The Government cannot blame global factors either. In contrast to the UK, headline inflation in the euro area has remained low and is expected to be just 2.1pc in October.

This divergence can be traced back to last autumn’s Budget. Rachel Reeves chose back then to raise businesses’ costs, notably by making it more expensive to hire people. These cost increases are being passed on to households through higher prices and fewer jobs.

October’s dip in inflation has lowered another barrier to the Bank of England’s next interest rate cut, probably next month. But relatively high inflation in the UK still means that the Bank has cut rates more slowly than it would otherwise have done.

The Labour Government likes to make a lot of the five rate cuts since the general election, which have lowered them to 4pc.

However, the European Central Bank has cut rates eight times in the current cycle, to just 2pc.

Above all, the main reason why UK interest rates are likely to be cut further is the weakness of economic activity and the prospect of another significant increase in the tax burden. Against this backdrop, the latest slight fall in inflation is little comfort.

So what could the Chancellor do about inflation next week? Reeves has said she intends to use the Budget to “cut NHS waiting lists, cut national debt and cut the cost of living”. The last of these is a clear signal that she will take some direct action to lower household bills.

The frontrunner appears to be the removal of the 5pc VAT from domestic energy bills. This might be popular, but it would also be poorly targeted – some of the biggest winners would be wealthier households who use more energy. It would also do nothing to address the fundamental reasons why energy costs in the UK are so high.

Moreover, the revenue loss would need to be offset elsewhere, most likely by increases in direct taxes. So while cutting VAT on domestic energy bills might reduce headline inflation, real disposable incomes could change little. In other words, some bills might be lower, but households would have less money to pay them.

These points would also apply to the transfer of green levies and other policy costs from household bills to general taxation. There would only be a net benefit to taxpayers if green subsidies were scaled back altogether.

Moreover, any reduction in headline inflation as a result of a cut in energy bills would only be temporary. The Bank of England’s chief economist, Huw Pill, has already said that the Monetary Policy Committee would look through it when setting interest rates.

If the Government is serious about bringing inflation down without crashing the economy, it should rethink policies that are driving up labour costs in both the public and private sectors. It should also drop policies that undermine market flexibility, including controls on the rental industry, more employment red tape and crazy energy policies.

Unfortunately, like many before it, this administration prefers headline-seeking gestures.

Julian Jessop is an independent economist

data.title: Eurostar needs more competition than just Branson’s Virgin
data.description:
There will be some cabin crew in bright red uniforms. There will probably be some loud music and plenty of dry ice. And Sir Richard Branson could almost certainly show up in a rocket, a balloon or perhaps even a submarine, pulling some kind of stunt or other.

We can expect plenty of glitzy razzamatazz when the first Virgin train heads out of London to the Continent in a few years’ time, breaking Eurostar’s monopoly of rail travel through the Channel Tunnel.

And yet we should not stop there. There should be lots of operators competing on that route – not just two.

After a long struggle, Virgin last week finally won its battle to run trains to the rest of Europe after securing approval from the Office of Rail and Road.

It will still need to get safety approvals from regulators in both the UK and the European Union, negotiate platform space and work out a deal with Eurostar. But it hopes to have a service to Paris, Brussels and Amsterdam up and running by the end of the decade.

For the first time in the three decades since the tunnel linking the UK to the rest of Europe was opened, passengers will finally be able to choose which company they want to travel with.

And at least one rail route in the UK will get some much-needed competition, even while the rest of the network is steadily nationalised.

It is about time. Eurostar has, just like any monopoly, become complacent and expensive.

When I checked on Friday afternoon, if I wanted to book a trip to Paris for the following weekend, the cheapest return train ticket would cost me £260 return, and well over £300 if I decided to travel at a civilised hour, while I could get a return easyJet flight for £110.

The prices are way too high, especially given that most of the infrastructure is now three decades old. It is not as if the service is amazing either, even if it recently unveiled spiffy new gender neutral uniforms for the staff.

On Trustpilot, the train service has an average review ranking of just 2.1 out of a possible 5.0, which suggests, to put it politely, that there is a little room for improvement.

That is not nearly good enough. The entire point of the tunnel was to replace flights with train routes, both to reduce congestion at the airports and to cut carbon emissions. The train service should have driven the budget airlines out of the market on that route by now.

Instead, eye-watering ticket prices mean many of us prefer to get a cheap flight instead. As for the plans for seamless train journeys that would link into the Continent’s increasingly sophisticated high-speed rail network, and whisk us all effortlessly around France, and on to Germany, Italy or Spain, they remain on the drawing board, and have done for decades.

It is a huge missed opportunity, and one that will only start to be fixed once the tunnel is finally opened up to competition.

Here is the problem, however. Why only allow Virgin to offer an alternative service? There were three more bidders for the route who were turned down.

As well as Virgin, Gemini Trains, chaired by the Labour peer Lord Berkeley, as well as Trenalitia, the main operating arm of the Italian rail operator and the Spanish start-up Evolyn, were all interested in bidding for the route.

Instead of just one new operator, we could have had four or five. Sure, there are issues with hangar space. But even in Sir Sadiq Khan’s London, where virtually nothing gets built any more, it is surely not impossible to build some extra storage capacity for the trains if that is what is needed, and if some extra platform space is needed at St Pancras, then surely that would be possible as well.

In reality, a whole series of competitors would genuinely kickstart the market. We need a range of different services: a British Airways-style business class offering, for example, for all the people travelling to Brussels or Paris on expenses, alongside a much cheaper Ryanair-style service for people who are getting away for the weekend and would rather spend their money on dinner out than the train fare.

Sure, not all of us want to make a seven or eight-hour rail trip to Milan, or take a 10-hour through-train to Madrid, but a few people will, partly because they want to cut their carbon footprint, or perhaps because they just happen, perfectly understandably, to prefer the old-fashioned romance of the train to the cattle-car conditions of most airports.

We could even see direct routes from other British cities, perhaps with trains running from Manchester straight through to Paris, or else from Birmingham direct to Brussels.

The important point is this: we can’t know for certain which routes would be profitable or what the levels of demand might be.

But we can be sure of one thing: if we allow lots of different operators access to the rail tracks running under the English Channel, then plenty of different possible routes will be tried out, and we will find out very quickly which ones work and which ones don’t.

A duopoly is a lot better than a monopoly, but only just. It can very quickly slip back into a cosy cartel, with both companies quietly agreeing to keep prices high and to keep the competition limited. It is less hassle and more profitable.

In reality, the tunnel has been a squandered asset. It should be opened up to a whole range of different services, and it will be far better off from a blast of fierce competition between competitors.

data.title: Leon scraps its coffee subscription
data.description:
Leon has cancelled its coffee subscription after its baristas struggled to keep up with the number of drinks they had to prepare.

For £25 per month, the Roast Rewards scheme allowed subscribers to claim up to five coffees per day as long as they were at least an hour apart. The subscription also gave members 20 per cent off the food menu.

However, John Vincent, the chain’s founder, said the scheme would be “retired” from Jan 1 as part of an initiative to “remove all the parts that don’t feel like Leon any more”. 

He said baristas were struggling to keep up with the number of coffees being ordered by subscribers.

In an email sent to a small number of the “most loyal Leon supporters”, Mr Vincent said the subscription was “getting in the way” of quality.

He wrote: “Two weeks ago, I returned to Leon, having sold the company back in May 2021. A lot has changed at Leon since then, and not all the changes have been positive. 

“Although Leon is clearly a business, owning Leon and looking after guests like you is a deeply personal endeavour. Helping people enjoy the natural health that is rightfully theirs is what I get most joy from.

“I aspire to return Leon to being a leader in food, and to re-establish quality as our number one priority. Our coffee subscription, Roast Rewards, is getting in the way of this mission. As a result, I have decided to bring it to an end. My decision is, I believe, the right thing for our guests, our teams, and the quality of the coffee we serve.”

A spokesman confirmed that the 21-year-old company would be “letting go of processes that make daily operations more difficult for its teams, which have in turn affected the big warm welcome it was once famous for”, adding: “Removal of Roast Rewards is part of John Vincent’s 108 initiative ‘to bring harmony back to everything we do’. 

“The number 108 symbolises balance and wholeness, which is what Leon is striving for: harmony in its food, its teams, and the planet.” 

The spokesman said staff would be enrolled in Wing Tsun training, a martial art, to improve coffee-making times “while lowering heart rates”.

Leon confirmed that all current Roast Rewards subscribers would receive a gift of Leon Club loyalty points when the subscription came to an end.

Last year, Pret a Manger prompted fury when it axed its “free drinks” for subscribers, almost four years after the deal was launched in the UK.

In a move that upset many customers, the chain said it was “time to rethink” the Club Pret offer, and that instead of providing five drinks each day and a 20 per cent discount on food for £30 a month, it would charge £10 for a subscription for half-price drinks.

data.title: Viva el ’Spoons: Wetherspoon to serve 6am pints and garlic prawns at first pub in Spain
data.description:
JD Wetherspoon is to open its first pub outside the UK and Ireland in the Spanish resort of Alicante.

The 1,000 sq ft watering hole, located in Alicante Airport’s departures hall, features a hacienda-style interior with an external terrace and will open its doors to drinkers in January.

Opening hours will be from 6am to 9pm, seven days a week, allowing travellers to get a beer with their breakfast before an early-morning departure.

Food will be served up to an hour before closing, with the menu featuring traditional Wetherspoon staples including burgers and pizzas, alongside local dishes such as garlic prawns and Spanish omelette.

Sir Tim Martin, the Wetherspoon founder, said the outlet, to be named the Castell de Santa Bàrbera, is the first of a number he plans to open overseas in the coming months.

He said: “We are very much looking forward to opening our first overseas pub. We believe it will be popular with people travelling home from Alicante.”

Expansion to Continental Europe comes as growth in the UK slows. Comparable sales grew by 3.7pc in the 14 weeks to Nov 2, down from growth of 5.9pc over the same period a year earlier.

Sir Tim criticised heavy taxes on pubs and fruit machines during an appearance on GB News on Friday. He complained of the “big tax differential between supermarkets and pubs”. 

He added: “Pubs have lost an alarming half their beer volumes to supermarkets since 2000, which accelerated during the lockdowns.”

Addressing next week’s Budget, Sir Tim said: “There are so many taxes of one kind or another. Wetherspoon generates about £1bn for the Government and our profits are about 6-7pc of what we pay in tax. Don’t kill the golden goose.”

Separately, travellers at Heathrow were advised to get breakfast at Wetherspoon rather than splash out three times as much at one of the hub’s private lounges.

Which?, the consumers’ group, said passengers awaiting their flight at Terminal 5 would be better off buying a £14.50 breakfast at the Wetherspoon Crown Rivers rather than stumping up £42 to eat at the Club Aspire lounge, which rated bottom in a survey of 28 paid-for lounges.

The pub breakfast includes two fried eggs, bacon, two Lincolnshire sausages, baked beans, three hash browns, mushrooms and three slices of toast, with tea or coffee for £3.50 extra.

The breakfast offering at the Club Aspire lounge featured congealed scrambled eggs and lacked extras such as mushrooms or tomatoes, according to Which?.

An undercover inspector also “complained of stained, dirty plates, and the floor was covered in crumbs, despite it only being the start of the day”, it said, with food “very poor, on the whole”.

The survey concluded that terminal lounges overall – not including those run by airlines – were “expensive and lacklustre”, making an airport pub or restaurant a better option.

Rory Boland, editor of Which? Travel, said: “There was once a time when an airport lounge was a special treat, but our latest results say quite the opposite.

“Instead of paying over £40 for a lukewarm buffet breakfast on a dirty plate, you can get a lot more for your money by visiting a bar or restaurant inside the airport.”

A spokesman for No1 Lounges, which operates the lounge in Terminal 5, said the Which? review was “‘disappointing” but reflected “only a brief moment in time and may not represent the experience of the thousands of guests who use the lounge every week”.

Wetherspoon operates eight pubs at UK airports, including two each at Heathrow and Gatwick.

Collinson Group, the owner of the Club Aspire lounge at Heathrow, was contacted for comment.

data.title: Millions of households to face higher water bills
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher-than-expected water bills after the competition watchdog allowed five utility companies to increase their charges.

Following a provisional ruling by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water will be able to increase bills by a collective £556m over the next five years.

While that is less than the £2.7bn requested by the companies, it will still lead to some households paying annual bills of up to £638 a year.

Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, said she understood the public’s anger over bill rises, but expected companies to offer support to those struggling to pay.

Between them, Anglian and Northumbrian have been allowed to raise bills by a further 1pc, while Southern, South East and Wessex have been handed increases of 3pc, 4pc and 5pc, respectively.

Together, they serve around seven million customers across the UK.

The ruling means that Anglian will charge an average bill of £599, while Northumbrian bill payers will pay £495. South East Water will increase bills to £322, while Southern will charge £638.

Wessex was handed the biggest increase of 5pc, meaning its bills will rise to £622. All had asked for larger increases.

Appeal to the CMA

It comes after the companies appealed to the CMA earlier this year, requesting permission to increase their bills higher than Ofwat, the water regulator, had previously ruled.

In defence of its decision, the CMA said the extra money would help reduce pollution and strengthen supply, although it also said it would help cover the companies’ rising debt costs.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said: “We’ve found that water companies’ requests for significant bill increases, on top of those allowed by Ofwat, are largely unjustified.

“We understand the real pressure on household budgets and have worked to keep increases to a minimum, while still ensuring there is funding to deliver essential improvements at a reasonable cost.”

Thames Water, Britain’s biggest water company, had also appealed, but its proceedings have been put on hold while it battles to stave off nationalisation.

Ms Hardy said efforts were being made to restore the public’s trust in water companies.

She said: “We’ve made sure that investment cash goes into infrastructure upgrades, not bonuses, and we’re creating a tough new regulator to clean up our waterways and restore trust in the system.”

It comes amid growing anger over the state of the water sector, which has been responsible for a rise in sewage failures in recent years.

Mike Keil, the chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), said: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.

“CCW’s own analysis suggests there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs, cutting bills by around £41 a year, but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return.

“There is a danger the customers of these companies will end up paying more, without seeing any additional improvements in return.”

Joanna Elson, the chief executive of Independent Age, a charity, said higher bills would be “extremely damaging to people on low incomes living in these areas”.

She said: “The people in later life we support tell us the drastic lengths they are taking to reduce their water costs, including only washing a couple of times a week, not flushing the toilet and cutting down on laundry. This shouldn’t be happening in modern Britain.”

Ms Elson added: “We urge the UK Government to protect customers on low incomes from rising water bills by establishing a national water social tariff. This will end the postcode lottery.

 “It’s not right that your address dictates how much financial support you receive.”

data.title: Labour forced to use pensioners to build thousands of homes
data.description:
Labour is relying on an army of “Bob the Builder” pensioners to deliver its target of 1.5 million new homes by 2029, a new report has warned.

The study by the Centre for Social Justice (CSJ) warned “Bob the Builder is heading for retirement” as it found the number of construction workers aged 65 or over has nearly tripled from 49,361 to 130,791 since 2008 and now accounts for one in 20 of all employees in the industry.

It warned the ageing profile of construction workers, with more than one in three now over 50, threatened to exacerbate a recruitment crisis that could derail Labour’s housebuilding plans. By 2050, the average age of a construction worker will be over 46 years old, according to the CSJ.

The report estimated that the target of 1.5 million new homes by 2029 will require an additional 161,000 workers, rising to over 239,000 once factoring in wider projected demand by 2030.

But the think tank warned this housebuilding target was extremely unlikely to be achieved as the construction sector’s “workforce has concurrently aged and diminished in size”.

There are now some 250,000 fewer builders than there were before the Covid-19 pandemic and Brexit. With many likely to quit the industry because of age, construction is relying on a diminishing number of younger workers without sufficient skills training programmes to replenish the industry.

Compared with 2008, there are two-fifths fewer 16 to 24-year-olds working in construction, a fall of more than 150,000. This trend has accelerated since before the Covid-19 pandemic, with over 30,000 fewer 16 to 24-year-olds in the industry compared with six years ago.

The CSJ blamed previous governments for attempting to “plug gaps” through immigration, saying that “the reliance on migration has contributed to a failure to tackle the root causes of the domestic construction workforce’s long-term decline”.

It said the root causes were “a dysfunctional industry labour model” – where workers were laid off and then left the industry for good every time there was a recession – and “a broken education and training system which fails to equip learners with the skills they need to enter employment”.

The CSJ recommended that the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) should introduce a workforce credit – a £670m effective tax cut worth 30 per cent of a young construction worker’s salary, paid for by reforming health benefits.

It said the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG) should go further and faster on planning reform by rolling out design programmes to get Britain building.

The Department for Education (DfE) should bring construction into secondary schools by introducing a new technical and vocational option at GCSE level, it said. The DfE, DWP and MHCLG should launch a nationwide ‘earn as you train’ campaign to upskill economically inactive people, and low-skilled construction workers.

The CSJ said the Home Office should resist calls by some firms in the construction industry to relax immigration rules and re-introduce the resident labour market test, where companies are required to advertise a job first in the UK before seeking to hire overseas.

Josh Nicholson, senior researcher at the CSJ, said: “Fixing Britain’s construction-skills shortage has never been more urgent. The construction workforce is in near-terminal decline, but today we are publishing a plan to turn this crisis around.

“Unless the Government and industry can work together to reverse these trends, then it is highly unlikely Britain will be able to build the housing and infrastructure it so desperately needs.”

Jonathan Hinder, the Labour MP, said: “Britain’s prosperity depends on its construction workforce, yet we lack workers by the thousands. For too long Britain has undervalued the contribution of construction workers to our society and economy.

“The Government wants to ‘build, baby, build’. To do so we need workers, workers, workers. Thousands of working-class young people, especially boys, could have their life chances transformed through a rewarding career in construction if we get this right.”

data.title: Barclays accused of closing entrepreneur’s bank account over OnlyFans work
data.description:
Barclays has been accused of shutting the bank account of an entrepreneur because of her work on the adult website OnlyFans.

Madelaine Thomas, who runs a tech start-up while also making income by selling adult content, said the high street lender refused to open a new account for her business and had closed a separate account related to her work on OnlyFans and other websites.

Ms Thomas had applied for a business bank account with Barclays for her start-up, Image Angel.

The company’s technology applies digital watermarks to online images, allowing their creator to track them and identify the culprit if they are distributed or shared with others. Ms Thomas set up the company after private photos of her were distributed without her consent.

Barclays’ Eagle Labs tech programme receives government support to nurture tech start-ups, including running a “female founder accelerator”.

Ms Thomas said she had initially received positive feedback when applying for a business bank account with Image Angel, but later received a series of questions about a separate bank account that received the income from her online adult work.

She said she had also received questions about a joint account she shares with her husband, which she uses to pay nursery fees.

“It’s crazy that I’m trying to get a business bank account for technology that is going to change the landscape in terms of violence against women and girls and yet I’m now being investigated for, I don’t even know what,” she said.

“The technology I’m creating is just protecting people. It’s a benefit to society.”

Writing on LinkedIn she said: “Barclays don’t like that I earn via platforms like OnlyFans.”

OnlyFans has almost five million creators and more than 350 million users. Ms Thomas is not the only OnlyFans creator who has had their Barclays account affected. The model Jessica Alves also said her account was deleted by the bank in 2021.

Last year, the Financial Conduct Authority warned banks against denying access for adult workers.

“As adult entertainment industry representatives explained to us, these account denials or terminations could lead to significant harm for individuals running those businesses,” the regulator said at the time.

Barclays said: “We have a responsibility to understand the source of funds when banking a business, regardless of the nature of that business.

“Where it is not possible to do so, we will examine each business on a case by case basis and we will only close a customer’s account after careful thought and in line with our product terms and conditions. We do not take this decision lightly, understanding the difficulties that having banking services withdrawn can cause.”

Ms Thomas said she had been able to open a business account with another bank.

data.title: Higher water bills are a shameless reward for failure
data.description:
On the face of it, the news that water suppliers won’t be allowed to ramp up bills by as much as they were hoping is cause for celebration.

At first glance, it looks like an all-too-rare rebuff for an industry that has been thumbing its nose at customers for far too long – which is, of course, precisely how this latest sorry charade is meant to be interpreted.

Ministers are desperate for us all to think that after decades of wrongdoing, it has taken a Labour government to finally get tough with the cowboys that pollute our rivers and charge us a fortune for the privilege, all while absentee shareholders and negligent executives line their own pockets with money that could’ve been spent on investment.

Yet, that isn’t what is happening here. Not even close.

Yes, bills won’t be increasing to the extent that suppliers were asking for – but the point is that an independent panel of “experts” has still sanctioned further rises beyond what Ofwat had approved and most objective observers thought those rises were already pretty steep.

So the idea that this is somehow a big victory for households and a defeat for water companies is profound nonsense. The opposite is true.

In fact, it is impossible to see further bill hikes as anything other than a reward for failure for an industry that has long treated customers and the environment with complete contempt.

Not that Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, seems even remotely prepared to acknowledge what is really happening here.

“We are laser-focused on helping ease the cost-of-living pressure on households. We’ve frozen fuel duty, raised the minimum wage and pensions and brought down mortgage rates, putting more money in people’s pockets,” she said – though hopefully not with a straight face.

This is gaslighting territory. It takes a special kind of shamelessness to boast about making people better off at the precise moment that millions of families are having a fresh round of bill rises imposed on them.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said on Thursday it is allowing five water companies – Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, Southern Water, South East Water and Wessex Water – to lift bills by up to 5pc over the next five years.

That might seem fairly tame if it wasn’t for the fact that it comes on top of an average increase of 24pc that industry regulator Ofwat had already approved in April.

As Mike Keil, chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), pointed out: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.”

In fact, two in five of the households these companies serve say they will struggle to afford the increases coming down their pipe, according to the CCW, which represents water customers across England and Wales.

What’s more, the CCW has conducted its own analysis showing there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs to cut bills by around £41 a year – “but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return,” Keil said.

However, you would’ve thought otherwise when reading Hardy’s hollow words on Thursday.

“I understand the public anger over bill rises – that’s why I expect every water company to offer proper support to anyone struggling to pay,” she said.

But as Keil said: “Three of these five companies also have no plans to end water poverty by 2030.”

The CMA doesn’t come out of this much better. It makes a big song and dance about how the additional funding will ensure “essential improvements”, such as more resilient supply and a reduction in pollution, are delivered “at reasonable cost”.

Most people will accept some level of inflation in their household expenses but I’m willing to bet that few regard a 27pc surge as reasonable – even if it is over five years.

Some customers are already grappling with rises close to, or more than, 50pc once inflation is added because suppliers have been outrageously front-loading increases to help pay for long-overdue infrastructure improvements.

The reality is that bill-payers are being asked to subsidise an era of playing catch-up because water suppliers failed in the past to keep pace with investment, maintenance and environmental duties.

The money was there but instead of being spent modernising the system, repairing antiquated pipes and ensuring sewage wasn’t pouring into our waterways, it was being lavished on greedy investors and weak-kneed bosses or vanishing straight out the door in the form of heavy debt repayments.

Research from the University of Greenwich has found that 35p in every pound spent on customer bills is swallowed up in debt and dividend costs.

With the system buckling, it is surely no coincidence that these are the highest bill increases since privatisation.

It is also no surprise that the suppliers that went crying to the CMA are also among the most debt-laden in a sector that has long been leveraged up to the gills.

Meanwhile, the competition policeman quietly concedes that alongside a much-trumpeted round of additional investment, bill increases will also help pay down debt and boost returns for investors – many of whom are the same faceless funds that presided over the current wretched state of affairs in the first place.

For all the talk of long overdue reform and a new era of genuine accountability from Labour, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that our broken water industry has effectively been handed a huge taxpayer-funded bailout.

data.title: Britain risks losing access to the world’s best drugs, warns AstraZeneca
data.description:
NHS patients risk losing access to the world’s best medicines unless the UK spends more on developing new treatments, the head of Britain’s largest drug group has warned.

Sir Pascal Soriot, chief executive of AstraZeneca, said the NHS could be forced to rely on cheaper, older medicines unless the Government changes course and spends more on developing new treatments.

Countries including the UK risked only being “able to afford generics” in the future if they continued to squeeze spending on innovative medicines, he said.

Generics are older drugs which are less cutting-edge, but have fallen off patent and so can be made more cheaply.

Sir Pascal said Britain was “hitting the bottom” in terms of its budget for medicines.

Currently, the UK spends around 9pc of its overall healthcare budget on branded medicines. Of that, only 7pc is spent on innovative medicines. It means the UK significantly lags other G7 countries.

The UK’s expenditure on new innovative medicines currently stands at just 0.28pc of GDP – around a third of the United States’s proportionate spending of 0.78pc of GDP.

“How low can you go?” Sir Pascal said. “Technologies are going to the US and they’re going to China and other parts of the world, so in 15 or 20 years, Europe could easily lose its health sovereignty.”

It will add to pressure on the Government after a wave of drug companies recently scrapped UK investments, citing unattractive NHS clawbacks and access to patients.

AstraZeneca earlier this year paused a £200m investment that would have expanded its research and development operations in Cambridge and drug bosses have argued that the UK is now “uninvestable”.

In September, Lord Vallance, the science minister, admitted that the NHS would have to spend more on new medicines to stop a wave of investment leaving Britain.

However, reports have suggested that the Treasury is reluctant to agree to a larger overall budget, pushing for any increase to be funded out of the existing allotted departmental cash.

Sir Keir Starmer has recently intervened in the row, with plans to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. In theory, this could allow the NHS to buy billions of pounds more in new drugs.

However, Sir Pascal said the Government also needed to reduce the size of NHS medicine clawbacks – or drug makers would be forced to pay more in NHS rebates in response to the rising medicine spend.

He said: “Ultimately, if you get a better price and then a year later, a big [rebate] rate, it doesn’t really help anything.”

It follows mounting pressure on Labour to offer a better drug pricing deal to pharmaceutical companies.

Earlier this week, Warren Stephens, the US ambassador, warned that more drug firms “will not only cancel future investments, they will shut down their facilities in the UK” if ministers did not quickly change drug prices.

He said: “This would be a major blow to a country that prides itself, rightly so, on its life sciences sector.”

data.title: Households face surprise increase in energy bills from January
data.description:
Energy bills are set to rise by £3 from January despite forecasts that households were set to benefit from a fall in prices.

On Friday, the energy regulator Ofgem set its price cap – the amount suppliers are allowed to charge their customers – at £1,758 per year for a typical household, up from its current cap of £1,755.

The rise comes despite forecasts that energy bills would fall next year, in a fresh blow to Ed Miliband’s pledge to bring prices down.

It follows two quarters of increases and comes amid fears that bills will rise further in April. The Government said it knew that bills were “too high”.

Ofgem said the increase in January’s price cap was “driven by government policy costs and operating costs”. Funding for Sizewell C, the new nuclear power plant, will add around £1 a month to bills and the regulator said the warm home discount – which knocks £150 off bills for poor households – would also add to costs.

By contrast, Ofgem said wholesale energy prices had fallen by 4pc in recent months and were stable. Wholesale gas prices in Europe fell to an 18-month low on Friday amid hopes for a peace deal in Ukraine and milder weather on the continent.

January’s rise is a shock, with most forecasters predicting a fall ahead of the announcement. Analysts at Cornwall Insight said earlier this week that they expected annual bills to drop to £1,733 in January.

It said bills would increase in April to £1,808, largely because of network charges on bills which are partly funding net zero upgrades to electricity infrastructure. In a further blow to consumers, analysts at the consultancy also warned this was “likely to be an enduring trend”.

Simon Virley of KPMG said Labour was under pressure to demonstrate to households how bills could come down.

“Beyond January, without any government intervention, the price cap is widely expected to rise again in April due to increasing network charges, and new subsidy costs – not just for renewables, but also Sizewell C” he said.

“The pressure remains on the Government to show how its clean power plan is reducing bills; the spotlight is now firmly on next week’s Budget and what measures could be announced to bring down energy bills.”

Sam Richards of Britain Remade added: “While a £3 increase in the energy price cap may not sound like much, the cost of living remains a major concern for millions of households across the country who have endured eye-watering energy bills for far too long.

“The Government risks making renewables more expensive by cramming as many projects as possible into the next renewable energy auction in order to hit its pledge of a clean grid by 2030 at all costs.”

Cornwall Insight says that wholesale energy prices, once the dominant driver of bills, are now forecast to drop to less than 40pc of the total price cap and remain below that threshold for the rest of the decade.

Instead, so-called non-wholesale costs – including network charges and green levies – are set to dominate, accounting for more than 60pc of the total.

On current trends, a separate analysis this week predicted this will push a typical household’s annual bill up by almost 17pc, or about £160, by 2030. This will be the case even if wholesale costs fall from their current level today, according to independent energy analyst Ben James.

Mr Miliband, the Energy Secretary, has previously promised to cut consumer bills by £300 a year – and recommitted to this promise when challenged recently.

However, senior executives from Octopus Energy, British Gas owner Centrica, E.On, EDF and Ovo urged ministers to urgently address levies that pay for net zero-related costs – which they said are one of the main factors driving bills higher.

They warned household energy bills were on course to rise by hundreds of pounds this decade because of ballooning levies.

Rachel Fletcher, of Octopus Energy, said: “If we continue on the path that we’re on right now, in all likelihood, electricity prices for a typical customer are going to be 20pc higher in four or five years’ time than they are now.

“And that’s even if wholesale prices halve.”

Claire Coutinho, the Conservative shadow energy secretary, said: “Ed Miliband promised to cut everyone’s energy bills by £300 but more and more experts are sounding the alarm that his plans will lock us into paying higher bills for decades.

“Despite gas prices falling, independent experts, energy suppliers, and academics say it’s the extra costs of Ed’s Net Zero targets that are putting upward pressure on bills.

“This week we had a report that green levies on bills will soar by another £260 by 2030. We simply cannot afford this. Cheap energy has to come first.”

Martin McCluskey, the energy minister, said: “We know that energy bills remain too high. That is why we are taking immediate action, with millions more families receiving £150 off their bills through the expanded warm home discount scheme this winter.

“We are taking the long-term action needed to bring down bills for good with the government’s clean power mission. We are also delivering our new golden age of nuclear, with cheaper, clean electricity to power millions of homes, kick-start economic growth and create thousands of jobs.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Labour plots blocks of flats near green belt train stations
data.description:
Labour is plotting a green belt building blitz with plans to make it far easier to build blocks of flats near commuter stations.

Steve Reed, the Housing Secretary, is preparing to grant default approval for building projects near well-connected train stations.

The new rules will extend to land within the green belt and include minimum housing density standards, implying properties must contain multiple dwellings.

The housing department said this was intended to “make the most of sustainable growth opportunities for local housing, jobs and businesses”.

Councils will be forced to inform the Government if they intend to block a project delivering 150 homes or more, giving ministers the opportunity to step in and overrule the decision.

As part of the plans, ministers are also preparing to scrap rules that require Sport England, The Gardens Trust and Theatres Trust to be consulted on construction projects.

Mr Reed said: “We’re making it easier to build well-connected and high-quality homes, using stronger powers to speed things up if councils drag their feet, and proposing to streamline the consultation process to cut back delays.”

Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, said: “We’re ending years of dither and delay by green lighting affordable new homes for working people.”

‘Deeply concerning’

However, the proposals sparked an immediate backlash from countryside and heritage groups.

John Watkins, chair of The Gardens Trust, said: “Precious parks and gardens, which have both grown and developed over many centuries, would be lost to communities both now and in future.

“It is also incredibly frustrating and disappointing that after intimating that the launch of this consultation was imminent back in February, the Government has delayed, delayed and delayed again – creating a huge amount of uncertainty and distraction.”

Mr Watkins said the trust would “robustly contest the proposal”.

Becky Pullinger, head of land use planning at The Wildlife Trusts, called the plans “deeply concerning”.

“Whilst we must seek opportunities to build homes that integrate with sustainable transport and existing infrastructure, local authorities must be enabled to do this – not ordered to do so, regardless of local circumstance,” she said.

“This erosion of local democracy will not only strip away communities’ ability to have a say in the types of homes and infrastructure they need, but also threatens to override the safeguards in place which protect the wildlife, nature and greenspaces local residents know and love.”

Alice Roberts of CPRE, the countryside charity, said the decision was “disastrous” for traffic congestion, adding that it contradicted the Government’s net zero ambitions.

She said: “Evidence shows the vast majority of trips in such developments will be made by car as the train only goes in a specific direction and stops only at specific locations.

“It is hypocritical for the Government to say it wants ‘sustainable’ development and then green light car-dependent development in this way.”

David Simmonds MP, shadow housing minister, said: “It’s clear Britain needs more homes. But instead of trying to work with local communities to deliver homes people actually want, Labour are trying to railroad through unpopular developments.”

The new proposals come as Mr Reed strives to meet Labour’s manifesto pledge to build 1.5 million homes by the end of parliament. Housebuilders have said they do not believe the Government will meet the target.

data.title: Asda sells off supermarkets for almost £600m as it scrambles to reduce debt pile
data.description:
Asda has sold off dozens of supermarkets as its private equity owners race to reduce a £3.8bn debt pile.

The struggling retailer, majority owned by TDR Capital, said on Thursday it has struck a £568m deal with two buyers to offload 24 supermarkets and one depot, in the latest move to raise funds from its estate.

Under the agreement, Asda is selling 20 stores and a depot in Leicestershire to Blue Owl, the Wall Street private credit group, for £467m. Ten of those have been purchased by Blue Owl through a joint venture with Supermarket Income REIT. 

Another 10 sites have been sold to investment managers DTZ for £101m.

Asda will continue to occupy the sites through a so-called sale-and-leaseback, with the retailer signing up to a 25-year lease with an option to extend for another 10.

An Asda spokesman said: “Asda’s property strategy is centred on maintaining a strong freehold base while also taking a considered and selective approach to unlocking value from our estate where appropriate.”

They said the deals “reflect that approach, enabling us to realise value from the sites while retaining full operational control”.

The spokesman added that the proceeds would be used to “fund our ongoing capital investments in the business and reduce net leverage”.

Asda is expected to use part of the cash to help repay a looming debt repayment to its former American owner Walmart.

According to Moody’s, the company has around £1bn of liabilities to address by 2028, including a £900m bill owed to Walmart.

Timo Fittig, a Moody’s analyst, said the deal “enables the company to address upcoming debt and preference share maturities with the proceeds raised”.

However, he said it would “increase its long-term debt burden through the additional leases”, warning that the lease liabilities would likely exceed the amount of proceeds raised.

Mr Fittig said: “As such, the transaction will likely increase the company’s total debt position, including leases, even if it uses most of the proceeds to repay debt.”

Some of the funds are also expected to go into Asda’s turnaround drive as Allan Leighton, its chief executive, comes under mounting pressure to win back customers.

In March, Mr Leighton said he had been given a “war chest” to kickstart a proposed turnaround, hailing plans to invest in price cuts, product availability and improving stores.

However, his revival has yet to yield results, with the latest industry figures indicating that Asda’s market share is at a record low.

Worldpanel figures earlier this month showed Asda’s sales slump deepened in the 12-week period to Nov 2, meaning its share of the market slipped to 11.8pc from 12.7pc 12 months ago when Mr Leighton took charge.

Despite the deal freeing up more cash for Asda, Shore Capital analyst Clive Black said: “I do not believe that Asda will have wanted to undertake further sale and leaseback activity.”

“Chipping away at that asset base may reduce indebtedness but it also increases operational gearing as it grows the annual rent roll.

“If Asda was trading well, that would not be a matter of great concern, but it clearly is not.”

The deals also threaten to increase its exposure to the private credit market through Blue Owl.

Earlier this week, Blue Owl called off a merger of two of its private credit funds amid mounting scrutiny over potential cracks appearing in the so-called shadow banking sector.

Shares in Blue Owl are down 26pc over the last six months.

data.title: Daily Mail owner in exclusive talks to buy The Telegraph for £500m
data.description:
The Daily Mail owner Lord Rothermere has entered exclusive discussions to acquire The Telegraph for £500m.

Lord Rothermere’s holding company DMGT said it “plans to invest substantially in Telegraph Media Group with the aim of accelerating its international expansion”.

The company is in talks with RedBird IMI, the joint venture between the United Arab Emirates and the US private equity firm RedBird Capital Partners.

They made their own tortured two-year bid for control of The Telegraph that collapsed last week amid continuing concerns over potential foreign state influence.

Lord Rothermere had lined up as a minority investor alongside RedBird and the UAE, and had been expected to take a stake of just 10pc. DMGT has longstanding business connections in the UAE via its oil and gas information arm.

DMGT said its takeover would comply with regulations on foreign state influence as “there will be no foreign state investment or capital in the funding structure”.

The planned addition of The Telegraph to DMGT’s existing portfolio, which, as well as The Daily and Sunday Mail titles, includes the i, Metro and New Scientist, is likely to trigger calls for an in-depth investigation of the impact on media plurality. The acquisition could leave Lord Rothermere in control of around half the national newspaper market.

DMGT said it was “confident any regulatory process can be concluded swiftly and positively”.

“Today’s media landscape is unrecognisable from a decade ago. News publishers have to compete against both vast global online platforms and myriad digital and social media news sources, some of them highly unreliable,” the group said in a statement.

‘I have long admired The Daily Telegraph’

Sky is expected to make similar arguments about the rise of tech giants in its ongoing attempted takeover of ITV’s broadcasting business, although concerns there mostly surround competition in advertising sales rather than the public interest in plurality of ownership.

The swift announcement of an agreement between RedBird, the UAE and DMGT comes days after peers demanded that Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, impose a transparent and independent sale process to guard against further uncertainty.

They echoed concerns raised in a Telegraph leader column that a foreign state should not be allowed to decide its next owner. It had emerged that a “poison pill” debt threatened to leave The Telegraph in unsustainable debt to the UAE.

Lord Rothermere said: “I have long admired The Daily Telegraph. My family and I have an enduring love of newspapers and for the journalists who make them. The Daily Telegraph is Britain’s largest and best quality broadsheet newspaper, and I have grown up respecting it.”

He expressed support for The Telegraph’s editorial leadership, which had objected to RedBird IMI.

“Chris Evans is an excellent editor, and we intend to give him the resources to invest in the newsroom. Under our ownership, The Daily Telegraph will become a global brand, just as The Daily Mail has,” he said.

A spokesman for RedBird IMI said: “DMGT and RedBird IMI have worked swiftly to reach the agreement announced today, which will shortly be submitted to the Secretary of State.”
