data.title: Paramount wins rights to show Champions League games in UK
data.description:
Paramount has triumphed in an auction to broadcast Uefa Champions League matches in the UK.

The US media giant, which is owned by the billionaire Ellison family, has secured the media rights to air the European football competition for four years from 2027.

The swoop signals the biggest shake-up of the sports broadcasting market for years, with Paramount dethroning TNT Sports, which currently holds the rights to the majority of games.

One source close to the auction described the US company’s bid as a “knockout”, saying it had paid a “very large” premium to secure the rights.

European clubs had been hoping to secure at least €5bn (£4.4bn) annually from the latest package of rights, representing an increase of 10pc.

The current package of rights, which was awarded in 2024, is worth around £2.9bn a year. In addition to the UK, Paramount has also secured rights to broadcast the competition in Germany.

Champions League matches are played on Tuesdays and Wednesdays, with Amazon thought to have retained its so-called “first pick” rights for the former.

Paramount’s winning bid is believed to have taken rival suitors by surprise.

Alongside TNT Sports, which is jointly owned by Warner Bros Discovery and BT, the auction was expected to attract bids from DAZN, the sports streaming service owned by the billionaire industrialist Sir Leonard Blavatnik.

Disney and Netflix had also been mooted as potential candidates as the competition sought to entice global streaming giants.

Paramount is expected to broadcast Champions League matches on its streaming platform, Paramount+, which launched in the UK three years ago.

The media giant could also air some games or highlights on Channel 5, its free-to-air broadcaster in the UK.

Paramount’s channel CBS already has rights to show Uefa Champions League matches in the US, where coverage is hosted by the London-based team of former footballers Thierry Henry, Micah Richards and Telegraph Sport columnist Jamie Carragher.

The live show, which is presented by Kate Scott, the former Sky Sports News broadcaster, provides social media clips for UK audiences but is not currently available to watch live outside of the US. Paramount’s acquisition of the domestic rights raises the prospect of the quartet playing a role in future UK coverage.

Paramount was also behind Sir David Beckham’s watch-along series “Beckham & Friends Live”, which saw guests including Tom Cruise and James Corden join the former England captain for live shows.

A source close to Paramount acknowledged the company’s bid was “competitive” but insisted it would make a strong return on its investment, both through advertising and signing up new subscribers to its streaming service.

The outcome will deal a heavy blow to TNT Sports, which has lost its prime package of sports rights in the UK. However, the broadcaster still holds Premier League rights until 2029, while it recently won a four-year deal to show the FA Cup.

It marks the latest example of aggressive empire-building by Paramount since its $8bn takeover by Skydance this summer.

The media behemoth, which owns Hollywood film studios and TV channels including CBS is led by David Ellison, son of Oracle founder Larry Ellison.

RedBird Capital, the US private equity firm that last week withdrew its bid to take control of The Telegraph, is its second-largest investor.

Paramount has already beefed up its ownership of prime media rights, splashing $7.7bn to secure exclusive rights to the Ultimate Fighting Championship in the US.

It also inked a five-year deal to stream the satirical cartoon series South Park, worth a reported $1.5bn.

In an even more audacious move, Paramount is now lining up an offer for Warner Bros Discovery, the owner of the Harry Potter film series and networks including HBO and CNN.

The US company has reportedly been in discussions with Middle Eastern sovereign wealth funds to raise cash for an improved offer after its first $60bn approach was rebuffed.

Paramount declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Renters’ Rights Act: What the new law means for renters and landlords
data.description:
The Renters’ Rights Bill has become the Renters’ Rights Act, after it received Royal Assent yesterday.

The Government must next set out its plans to implement the changes and bring the law into force.

Tenants who rent in the private sector will receive greater protections from eviction, unfair rent increases and greater stability of tenure.

The controversial body of legislation is the biggest change to renting in over 30 years, according to industry body The National Residential Landlords Association (NRLA).

Here, Telegraph Money explains the measures laid out in the bill, what that means for landlords and when it is likely to become law.

  • Renters’ Rights Act key points
  • What was the Renters’ Rights Bill?
  • New rules in the Renters’ Rights Act
  • How will it affect landlords?
  • Will it apply to existing tenancies?

Renters’ Rights Act key points

  • End of Section 21 “no fault” evictions: instead, landlords wanting to regain possession of a rental property will need to use approved grounds
  • Rent increases can only happen once a year: any increases must reflect market rates
  • New property standards: under the new “Decent Homes Standard”, properties must be safe, of good value and good quality
  • End of fixed-term tenancies: new and existing tenancies must change to rolling agreements
  • New landlord ombudsman and database: will give tenants access to impartial and binding resolution for complaints against landlords. All landlords must sign up
  • New pet rules: tenants have the right to request pets, but landlords can stipulate pet insurance must be taken out to protect from any resulting damage
  • Rental payment overhaul: landlords cannot accept offers above the advertised asking price for rentals, and no more than one month’s rent can be paid in advance.

What was the Renters’ Rights Bill?

The Renters’ Rights Bill built on the previous Renters’ Reform Bill, introduced by the Conservatives. Now the Renters’ Rights Act, it will introduce new protections and rights for the estimated 11 million private tenants in England by reforming the current system of renting.

An end to fixed-term tenancies, longer notice periods to give renters more time to find a new home and restrictions on rent increases will give tenants more rights and landlords less control over how they manage their property and buy-to-let business.

Labour says it will “transform the experience of private renting” which will largely be achieved by ending Section 21 evictions dubbed “no-fault evictions”.

The NRLA has argued the rules could force landlords out of the sector – reinforced by a survey earlier this year by Pegasus Insight, which revealed 37pc of landlords planned to sell a property in the next 12 months while just 6pc said they intended to buy.

New rules in the Renters’ Rights Act

Evictions and serving notice:

  • Section 21 no-fault evictions will be abolished.
  • Fixed-term tenancies will be replaced with periodic tenancies, also known as rolling contracts that don’t have an end date.
  • Tenants will have a 12-month protected period at the start of their tenancy preventing landlords from forcing evictions to move back in or sell.
  • Tenants can serve two months’ notice to quit the property at any time.
  • Landlords must give four months’ notice if they wish to move back in or sell a rental property.
  • Landlords will not be able to evict a tenant in arrears until three months’ payment have been missed, up from two months while the notice period has been increased from two to four weeks.

Prices and payment:

  • Rent increases are limited to once a year and only to the market rate, which can be challenged by tenants at the first-tier tribunal if they believe it is excessive. Increases cannot be backdated.
  • Large amounts of rent cannot be demanded in advance of a tenancy starting.
  • Landlords must register with a digital database, failing to do so means that they cannot use certain grounds of possession.
  • Asking rents must be published to prevent bidding wars, making it illegal for landlords to accept offers higher than the rent advertised.

Standards and safety:

  • The Decent Homes Standard and Awaab’s Law, currently applicable to homes in the social housing sector, will be extended to private sector landlords to ensure homes are made safe, are of good value and good quality.
  • A private rented sector landlord ombudsman will be set up to provide an impartial and binding resolution for tenant complaints against landlords.

Discrimination:

  • Strengthened rights for tenants to have pets, although landlords can insist on pet insurance to cover any damage to their property.
  • End discrimination against tenants on benefits or with children.

Compliance:

  • Councils will have the power to issue civil penalties of up to £7,000 to landlords who don’t comply with the reforms, rising to £40,000 for a serious breach or repeat non-compliance with the law.
  • More strength will be given to rent repayment orders that can be issued by councils when landlords breach the law.

How will it affect tenants?

The Renters’ Rights Act will give tenants greater protections and stronger rights when they’re renting.

As outlined in the rules above, among the changes the new law will usher in, tenants must be given longer notice periods when their landlords want to move back in or sell the property they’re renting, the property must legally reach the Decent Homes Standard, and they will no longer be subject to frequent rent increases.

For any tenants with a complaint about their landlord, the introduction of an independent ombudsman will help readdress the natural power dynamic and help tenants to reach a resolution.

How will it affect landlords?

Sylvie Harris, of property consultancy INHOUS, said: “Through the abolition of Section 21 no-fault evictions, landlords will only be able to end tenancies using specific grounds under Section 8, such as rent arrears or anti-social behaviour or if they intend to sell or move into the property.”

For the latter, an extended four-month notice period must be served.

But the pressure this will place on a court system, which Matthew Pennycook, the housing minister, acknowledged during a parliamentary debate was “on its knees”, is raising concerns and has led to an amendment being tabled to review the impact of this plan on the legal system.

When the Conservative government brought forward its draft renters rights legislation, then-minister Michael Gove said that the right to make no-fault evictions would remain in place until a full review of the courts had been completed.

Currently, the average time between a landlord submitting a claim for an eviction and regaining possession of their property is more than seven months. In response to the Government’s claims that the courts will be ready for the impact of the changes, Ben Beadle, chief executive of the NRLA, said: “Seven months is an eternity for responsible landlords who may be dealing with serious rent arrears and for neighbours having to endure anti-social behaviour.”

Not only could it take longer to evict a tenant, landlords will have to endure greater levels of arrears before they apply for possession as the minimum level of arrears for eviction is bumped up from two to three months.

Landlords operating in the student let sector could also face challenges in operating their businesses efficiently if the legislation is implemented in its current form.

Ending fixed-term tenancies removes the certainty for these landlords that their property will be available to let to a new batch of students when the academic year begins.

Although grounds for possession have been included, which allows landlords of houses in multiple occupation (HMOs) to evict last year’s students to make way for a new group, one and two-bedroom student flats were not mentioned.

Banning the receipt of large sums of rent upfront, meanwhile, could put landlords off renting to international students or tenants who have had credit difficulties in the past or do not pass affordability assessments. Without the security of payment in advance, these groups may struggle to be accepted.

When will the bill become law?

The Government has announced May 1 2026 as the date when Section 21 evictions will be outlawed in England. Also from this date, fixed-term tenancy contracts must be replaced by ‘rolling’ contracts, there’ll be an end to bidding wars and landlords must include clearer rules on pets.

Will it apply to existing tenancies?

Yes, it will. We are still waiting to find out the finer details, but the plan is to make the changes to tenancies in one go rather than create a two-tier system that applies the rules to new and existing tenancies at different times.

data.title: British Airways owner threatens to shift investment to Madrid amid Heathrow row
data.description:
The chief executive of British Airways (BA) has warned that its Spanish owner could ditch investment in Heathrow if the bill for the airport’s third runway is too high.

Sean Doyle said International Airlines Group (IAG) is concerned by the cost of Heathrow’s £49bn expansion, which will be largely paid for by carriers and passengers.

He said IAG does not support plans to move the M25 to accommodate the expansion, while he also urged officials to consider pursuing a shorter, cheaper runway.

A failure to keep passenger fees under control risks making Heathrow less attractive, Mr Doyle said, while it could also lead to IAG prioritising investment in its airlines overseas, including Spain’s Iberia and Ireland’s Aer Lingus.

He said: “We’re part of a group and the group allocates capital on the basis of performance.

“If Heathrow becomes uncompetitive and that impacts the performance of an airline like BA, there’ll be opportunities to put that capital elsewhere.”

Mr Doyle added: “Madrid is flying at the minute, with Iberia reporting very strong results, and Barcelona is an expanding market we have a very strong presence in. Dublin has built a new runway for a couple of billion, so there’s headroom there.

“There will be debates about competitiveness and airport charges when it comes to the way IAG allocates capital. We’re not at that point yet but that’s why, for everybody’s sake, a competitive Heathrow is very important.”

British Airways stepped up its campaign against escalating costs at Heathrow as the Government prepares to choose between competing runway plans by the end of the month.

Heathrow Airport’s own proposals call for the construction of a 3.5km runway built over the M25, lifting annual passenger numbers from 82 million to 150 million.

A rival plan from Surinder Arora, the hotel tycoon, envisages a 2,800m strip that would be unable to take the very biggest jets when fully laden.

Sir Keir Starmer and Rachel Reeves have put airport expansion at the heart of Labour’s bid to kickstart the economy, arguing that the pursuit of growth “trumps” net zero commitments.

Labour is seeking to speed up legal processes in a bid to secure planning permission for a third runway before the next general election.

Mr Doyle said BA accepts that Heathrow is full but questioned whether the airport’s own model is unnecessarily complex.

He said: “We need to make sure we’re competitive and build this runway as quickly and efficiently as we can, and any opportunity to achieve that goal should be explored.

“If you can avoid moving the M25, you should avoid moving the M25. You can’t argue against the logic of that. And I think we should look at ways of potentially building a shorter runway.”

‘Serious calculations’

Thomas Woldbye, Heathrow’s chief executive, hit back at the claims, dismissing Mr Doyle’s suggestion that building over the motorway may be unnecessary.

He said: “I agree with him – if we could avoid it, we should – but it is not avoidable. It’s expensive but it’s a well-controlled, known technology solution.

“If you build a shorter runway, you have to move more motorways, like the M4 spur, which is more complicated. It’s in several layers with a railway underneath and you have nowhere to put your motorways or your building site and soil.”

He said Mr Arora’s plan would require demolition of more than 2,000 homes, compared with only about 750 for Heathrow’s own proposals, and that the difference had not been costed.

Mr Woldbye said that could make a shorter runway more expensive and that “serious calculations” indicated that only a full-length runway would keep longer-term costs to a minimum.

He said: “You’d end up with much less capacity. That does not make sense. If you want lower fares, you need the maximum number of passengers to spread costs.”

The chief executive said that Heathrow would nevertheless look at any decision from Labour recommending a cheaper alternative to its blueprint.

Asked if the airport could guarantee that costs for expansion would be capped at £49bn, Mr Woldbye said that “naturally I cannot guarantee that” since the runway would not be ready for a decade and a contingency of 20-30pc “is normal”.

He added: “We have no reason to be more expensive than we should be. We are a regulated entity, our interest is to be as efficient as we possibly can.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The West can still win the electric car war with China
data.description:
China’s quest for world domination of electric vehicles (EV) and batteries is starting to falter. 

The West has every chance of winning the next round of this bare-knuckled industrial contest.

Creeping doubts haunted the World Power Battery Conference in Sichuan this month. It was not the celebration of total victory seen in recent years.

China is struggling to make the leap forward to solid state batteries and the next generation of advanced energy storage – ultimately expected to push the standard EV driving range above 500 miles while cutting weight, slashing charging times to 10 minutes, and eliminating the risk of thermal runaway.

“I think predictions about solid-state batteries are like fortune-telling,” said Wu Zhixin, vice chairman of China’s Solid-State Battery Platform.

Wu said researchers were nowhere close to mastering the complex metallurgy, and he doubted whether these batteries would be in commercial production in China much before the mid-2030s.

Deng Chenghao from Changan Automobile said the whole field was besieged by myths and hyperbole, driven by online EV fantasists with no idea what they were talking about.

“There are so many technical issues to be resolved, and we are still in the laboratory stage. We don’t know what the costs will be,” he told the forum.

Meanwhile, the mood was almost chipper at the recent Battery Show North America 2025 in Detroit. The halls were packed and the number of exhibitors jumped to 1,350 from the 1,150 a year earlier.

The industry seems to be looking beyond Donald Trump, and shrugging off the last hurrah of the combustion engine.

There was a hum of optimism over the latest lithium manganese-rich (LMR) batteries, which are being developed by Ford and GM – with Korea’s LG – and expected to hit the market by 2028.

These eliminate cobalt, which mostly comes from the Democratic Republic of the Congo and is controlled by China. They essentially halve the need for expensive nickel from Indonesia.

They use lots of cheap and ubiquitous manganese, a hard and brittle silvery metal, instead – but still surpass a 400 mile range. It has taken 10 years, but researchers have finally overcome the voltage loss and short life-cycle of manganese batteries.

The technology has effectively leap-frogged the lithium iron phosphate (LFP) batteries that have swept the mass market in China, and are entirely dominated by Chinese companies. The upstart US rival has an energy density one third higher but for the same cost.

The British start-up Anaphite in Bristol is the world pioneer in a new technique that slashes cell production costs 40pc by replacing the wet coating inside batteries with an electrode film made of dry power. China is not even close, and the invention is not easy to copy.

“It has a massive impact on energy costs,” said Dr Ben Walsh from the UK’s Battery Innovation Programme. “You don’t need 400 meters of ovens any more to dry off the moisture from the liquid coating. Anaphite really is a fantastic company with a technology that is all home-grown.”

We can debate whether China has locked up the global market and supply chain for lithium-ion batteries and is now uncatchable, but it has certainly not locked up the future of solid state batteries – whatever some sharp actors may claim in their press releases.

The West is competing toe to toe and may well pull ahead.

The UK’s solid-state champion Ilika has already begun an operational pilot line for its oxide solid electrolyte. It aims to cut £2,500 off the cost of a large battery pack and lower the weight of an EV by 100 kilograms.

There are of course formidable rivals in the US, Europe and the Far East, all chasing the enormous prize. BMW has teamed up with America’s Solid Power and Korea’s Samsung on a sulfide-based solid electrolyte, aiming to double energy density to 500 watt hours per kilogram (Wh/kg).

Amprius Technologies in California is also nibbling at 500 Wh/kg, a level that halves the weight of a battery and consigns range angst to the memory bin. But the UK is the thick of it at multiple stages of the chemistry supply chain.

“There is far too much doom and gloom about EVs and batteries in this country. We have really got our act together, with some of the best professors and universities anywhere,” said Dr Walsh.

“Europe is spending billions on this but it is not well coordinated. We’re actually way ahead at them, and people are taking notice around the world. We should be thinking about this as a massive opportunity for the UK to attract plants,” he added.

The danger for China is that it is sinking eye-watering sums into technologies that are no longer cutting-edge and is wasting resources on vast overcapacity that will never find a market.

China’s battery production capacity last year reached 1.17 terawatt hours, greater than the world’s entire demand. It is targeting 6.3 terawatt hours and 400 gigafactories by the end of the decade in one of the most extreme episodes of indiscriminate over-building in history.

Communist Party planners seemed to have learned little from their conquest of the global ship-building market, a strategy backed by subsidies, give-away energy, and opaque help of all kinds that has ended in a revenue bloodbath for loss-making behemoths.

China’s share of global ship sales is currently in freefall. The momentary dominance is slipping through their fingers.

George Magnus from Oxford University’s China Centre said this industrial policy has been gobbling up some 7 to 8pc of GDP. The market discipline of Schumpeterian clearance has been blocked, allowing industrial deformation to fester.

China’s leaders may also find that their ruthless, state-driven, decade-long drive to control global supplies of the minerals and materials needed for EV production is less useful than they thought, and in some cases completely useless. These metals are not all so critical after all.

China’s weaponisation of rare earths and its curbs on battery technology – not just in retaliation against Trump, but against the whole democratic world – has alerted everybody to its wolf warrior intentions.

You cannot replace the mask once you have ripped it off. American and European companies will guard their technological secrets more closely this time.

The danger for the West is that it will succumb to the siren voices of defeatism and start to believe that it is already too late to compete with China in the electrotech revolution.

The most certain road to economic decline is to do what China’s Qing Dynasty did in the 19th Century – but Japan did not do under the Meiji Restoration – which was to put up its head in the technological sand and cling to a dying order.

The West still has everything to play for in the long fight for EV supremacy, so long as we can all block out the deafening noise of the culture war.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Sky loses ad contract to Channel 4 after reeling from payment blunder
data.description:
Sky has lost an advertising contract to rival Channel 4 after an accounting blunder that cost the broadcaster hundreds of millions of pounds.

Channel 4 will take on responsibility for selling adverts and sponsorship on behalf of the Great Network, which includes the Great TV, Great Mystery, Great Action and Great Romance channels.

The public service broadcaster has poached the multi-year contract, which begins in January, from Sky Media via its ad sales arm Channel 4 Sales.

It comes a year after The Telegraph revealed that Sky had been forced to write massive cheques to Hollywood giants, including Warner Bros Discovery and Paramount, after discovering it had underreported revenues owed to its advertising partners since around 2017.

Sky Media carried out an internal inquiry into the blunder, leading to the departure of some members of staff.

The Great Network, which is owned by free-to-air broadcaster Narrative Entertainment, is the first advertising partner to cut ties with Sky following the saga, although there is no suggestion its decision was driven by Sky’s ad error.

A source close to Sky insisted the broadcaster made the decision not to continue its commercial partnership with Narrative after the end of the year. They added that Sky recently extended its deal with Warner Bros Discovery.

One source close to Narrative said that while Sky Media approached ad sales “by the numbers”, Channel 4 took a more creative approach, pointing to innovations such as the rollout of ads on the pause screen.

Advertising scrutiny

It also comes amid wider scrutiny of Britain’s TV ad sales market as Comcast, Sky’s US owner, weighs up a £1.6bn bid for ITV’s broadcasting division.

Any such tie up would combine the two large ad sales houses in the UK, giving them a combined market share of around 69pc. Channel 4 is the third largest player in the market with a 25.5pc share.

TV bosses have called on regulators to ease competition rules around TV advertising, arguing that broadcasters are now competing in a digital world with tech behemoths such as Google and Meta.

Nevertheless, the merger talks have sparked speculation that Sky could be forced to give up some of its third-party contracts.

The contract win comes as a boost to Channel 4, which has handled ad sales for UKTV, the BBC Studios-owned broadcaster behind channels such as Dave and Drama, since 2010.

The Great Network relaunched in September and now has an audience of 7.5 million monthly viewers across Freeview, Sky and Virgin Media.

Rak Patel, chief commercial officer at Channel 4, said: “This partnership with the Great Network underlines the unique appeal of Channel 4 Sales to advertisers, and, as a sales partner for other broadcasters.

“Stable, British-owned and brand-safe, we have a track record of award-winning innovation allied to a progressive advertiser strategy marrying sales and service.”

Paul Dunthorne, managing director of Narrative Entertainment, said: “Channel 4 have long been the creative leaders in both programming and ad sales, delivering new and compelling TV matched with innovation in the commercial arena.

“Since the inception of the Great channels, we have consistently beaten the market and grown our commercial impacts – and we’ve accelerated that growth with our September relaunch.”

Mr Dunthorne added: “Working with Channel 4 allows us to capitalise on that growth and deliver an even better service to our viewers and ad partners.”

data.title: Thames Water crisis threatens London super-sewer
data.description:
Thames Water’s debt crisis threatens to trigger millions of pounds in losses for London’s £4.5bn super-sewer.

Tideway Tunnel, which owns the project, has sounded the alarm ahead of a looming handover later this year – with bosses fearing Thames could withhold cash in its scramble to avoid nationalisation.

The sewer owner said it is closely monitoring Thames’s plight and has sought assurances from the Government to ensure the project will not be threatened if the company collapses into special administration.

In its latest annual report, Tideway said: “We continue to note Thames Water’s evolving financial position and this has led to concern that their situation might change.

“There is a possible risk that Thames Water, given its current financial position, or a special administrator may choose not to pay an element of Tideway’s revenue.

“This would not be consistent with Thames Water’s licence and would be expected to lead to a breach and enforcement action.”

As part of its contingency plans, Tideway also considered the possibility of Thames failing to support the sewer’s operations, warning that this “could affect our ability to deliver our investment programme on time and on budget.

“If Thames Water does not comply with the revenue agreement, it could have a financial impact,” the company added.

Concerns over Thames Water’s future come as the company struggles under the weight of a £17bn debt pile.

The company admitted last month that it could enter a taxpayer-backed special administration regime if a rescue deal led by more than 100 creditors fails to succeed.

Thames’s financial crisis is in stark contrast to the completion of Tideway, which has been hailed as a rare infrastructure success story.

The 25km tunnel under the River Thames, which became fully operational earlier this year, represents the biggest improvement to London’s sewage network in 150 years.

Buried 67m deep, the concrete pipe is designed to ease pollution pressures by redirecting sewage away from the river and into a treatment works in east London.

While Thames will not own Tideway, it is expected to be charged an annual fee for its use of the sewer. This will be paid to Bazalgette, a company bankrolled by a group of banks and pension funds.

A Tideway spokesman said: “Tideway is a completely separate, independent company set up to deliver the Thames Tideway Tunnel project.

“This vital new infrastructure is now operational, and has already diverted away nearly eight million tonnes of sewage that would have historically spilled into the Thames.

“We remain confident that Tideway’s revenues are well protected given water industry legislation and the licence obligations that apply to both Thames Water and Tideway. We continue to work effectively with Thames Water to deliver the benefits of the project.”

A Thames spokesman said: “Thames Water collects revenue from customers on Tideway’s behalf and passes it on to Tideway on receipt. Tideway’s revenues are well protected given water industry legislation and the license obligations that apply to both Thames Water and Tideway. We continue to work effectively with Tideway to deliver the benefits of the project.”

data.title: Britain faces threat of blackouts from ‘nuclear planning paralysis’
data.description:
Britain faces the risk of energy shortages because of a “planning paralysis” crippling the nuclear sector, a think tank has warned.

On Thursday, a report for Policy Exchange by former Cabinet secretary Lord Case said the UK faces growing threats from blackouts unless new nuclear power stations are granted fast-track approval to begin construction.

“The UK is barrelling toward an energy crisis – and an urgent change in policy is needed to stop costs continuing to rise, and to secure our energy security ... We must put nuclear enterprise at the heart of government reform,” said the report.

No new nuclear power stations have been built in the UK for 30 years, with the report warning that demand for power will surge over the next decade, just as many ageing nuclear and gas power plants are set to shut down.

It said urgent changes were needed to “cut through the planning paralysis” or risk an energy crisis by 2035.

“Without further nuclear power, Britain risks energy shortages that will drive up industry costs and raise household electricity bills,” the report added.

Lord Case and his colleagues list a set of legal changes they claim will make it faster and cheaper to build nuclear power stations.

These include a ban on environmental groups from taking legal action against nuclear projects because of the long delays such cases cause.

“Charities and pressure groups should be barred from bringing cases objecting to new nuclear projects – with only individuals or businesses directly affected allowed to do so,” said the report.

It also suggests that Parliament should use a “hybrid” act to authorise each new nuclear power station – automatically granting planning permission and ending the delays caused by judicial review.

Such moves would effectively strip out most forms of local oversight and citizen democracy from nuclear projects – even though they are among the UK’s most expensive infrastructure schemes, imposing decades of costs on taxpayers and consumers.

Shaun Spiers, the director of the Green Alliance, attacked the report for suggesting opposition to nuclear power should be constrained.

“We have seen growing attacks on civil society in so-called illiberal democracies like Hungary and Slovakia. It is sad to see Policy Exchange, which has had such a record influence over recent governments, suggesting these attacks should be extended to the UK,” he said.

A number of gas-fired power and nuclear stations are set to close over the next decade, and will be replaced by wind and solar plants backed up by battery farms.

The Government is also backing new nuclear but only has two stations under construction – Hinkley Point C and Sizewell C.

Last week, it confirmed the site of the former Wylfa nuclear power station on Anglesey as the location for the first of three Small Modular Reactors (SMRs) in the UK.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said: “We tabled radical amendments this year to completely exempt nuclear power sites from absurd environmental rules, and protect them from vexatious judicial reviews. Labour refused to back them.”

On Thursday, a separate report from energy consultancy Wood Mackenzie warned that Ed Miliband’s policy of blocking new oil and gas exploration licences will undermine UK and European energy security.

It shows that Europe collectively consumes 12.6 million barrels of oil a day, with Norway and the UK providing 2.5 million barrels – making Britain a key contributor to energy security across the region.

David Whitehouse, the chief executive of trade body Offshore Energies UK, said: “This shows the critical importance of the UK’s homegrown oil to its own economic and national security.”

A Department for Energy Security and Net Zero spokesman said blackouts were unlikely.

“These claims completely ignore that we are driving forward a golden age of nuclear power by backing Sizewell C in Suffolk and hosting Britain’s first Small Modular Reactor nuclear power station in Wylfa in Anglesey,” they said.

“In its 75-year history, there has never been a complete grid shutdown, and through our clean power mission, we will make the country even more energy secure – protecting households against volatile fossil fuel markets that we can’t control.”

data.title: France to sue British buyout firm for ‘short changing’ failed steelmaker
data.description:
The French government is preparing to sue a British private equity firm over claims it short-changed a failed local steelmaker.

Roland Lescure, the French finance minister, said the government would be “uncompromising” as he accused Greybull of failing to follow through on investment pledges for Novasco, which is based in Hagondange, a city in north east France.

The situation has led to months of uncertainty for the 700 people employed by Novasco, after the company was placed into administration in August – a year after it was taken over by Greybull.

As part of the 2024 deal, the British investment firm agreed to pump €90m (£79m) into the business alongside a cash injection by the French government. The French government has provided €85m of funding for Novasco’s four plants.

However, the finance ministry said Greybull had only provided €1.5m in investment since last year, piling pressure on the steelmaker, which produces materials for the automotive industry.

The company has been in a court-ordered administration for the past few months, with staff expected to learn their fate this week, as the court considers takeover offers. French newspapers said “significant” job cuts were likely under any deal.

Speaking on television network TF1, Mr Lescure criticised Novasco’s former owners, saying: “We are not here to nationalise industrial companies, we are here to accompany buyers.”

‘Sums don’t add up’

The French government accused Greybull of being a “rogue” buyer as it set out plans to take legal action against the company.

Mr Lescure said, on the Novasco investment, “the sums don’t add up”, adding: “When [buyers] do not meet their commitments, as is the case with Greybull, we will be uncompromising.”

The warning over the French steelmaker follows a series of failed investments for Greybull.

The turnaround firm previously owned the UK’s second largest steelmaker British Steel, buying the business from Tata for a nominal sum of £1 in 2016. At the time, it pledged a £400m investment to return British Steel back to becoming a “national champion, a reference point for European steel makers”.

However, three years later, British Steel was under “significant strain”, UK ministers said, requiring the Government to agree to a £120m loan.

Weeks after securing the loan, British Steel sought further emergency financial support from the Government to stay afloat.

Those talks collapsed, resulting in the state-backed Official Receiver stepping in to take control of the business. It placed the business into compulsory liquidation. British Steel was ultimately sold to Chinese steelmaker Jingye Group in 2020.

The problems at British Steel followed a run of failed investments for the UK turnaround firm, which also included the collapsed electrical chain Comet, convenience store My Local, snooker hall Rileys and the airline Monarch.

A spokesman for Greybull said: “We strongly believe that we have met all of our obligations as they fell due and we will demonstrate that in any legal proceedings if required. Financially, Greybull has suffered losses in the process.”

The spokesman added that the situation at Novasco had been “far worse than presented, and to an extent far beyond what one typically encounters”.

They added: “⁠This situation made it impossible for Novasco’s plans to be executed and NovAsco has faced huge industrial challenges which is the root cause of the issues, not lack of capital ...

“We have been in regular contact with the Government to explore ways to provide further support to the company and its employees. Yesterday, the Government informed us they were pursuing other avenues.”

data.title: Ozempic maker wins High Court ban on fake fat jab websites
data.description:
The drugmaker behind Wegovy has secured a major victory after the High Court granted it a blocking order for websites selling fake versions of its weight-loss jabs.

Novo Nordisk will be able to force internet providers to block access to four websites that the company accused of selling “fake and unauthorised forms of semaglutide”, the ingredient which helps people lose weight.

It claimed the websites were selling counterfeit versions of Ozempic, the drugmaker’s diabetes injection, which is used off-label for weight loss.

Novo Nordisk warned the counterfeit drugs “pose a risk to health” and were not regulated, adding: “The substances [the websites] offer to supply have not been evaluated by the UK MHRA [Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency] as being safe or of suitable quality.”

GPs have warned that people are “dicing with death” by using fake injections, with reports of drugs being wrapped up in kitchen roll without instructions when they are sent to customers.

Earlier this month, an investigation found copycat versions were being sold on Facebook and TikTok, with patients who took them reportedly losing vision and vomiting repeatedly. Both social media companies said the sale of weight-loss injections was prohibited under their policies, with TikTok removing searches and hashtags for the drugs after the investigation.

However, there are growing fears that more people could start hunting out dangerous fakes after a major jump in the price of one of the biggest approved obesity jabs.

In September, US drugmaker Eli Lilly increased the UK price of Mounjaro – known as the King Kong of weight-loss injections – by as much as 170pc following pressure from Donald Trump, the US president, to lower the cost for Americans.

It means that the drug now costs as much as £330 per injection pen, based on the highest dose. Each injection pen includes four doses. Previously, the highest-dose injection pen cost £122.

Drug crime specialists at the time warned that the increase was likely to lead to a jump in the number of copycat drugs being imported from Chinese labs and sold illicitly in the UK.

Novo Nordisk has not increased its prices. At an online pharmacy, patients can expect to pay between £99 and £199 for four doses.

It shipped in more supply of its weight-loss jabs to Britain following the price increase at its rival, after seeing more demand.

A spokesman for Novo Nordisk said of the blocking order: “We are pleased that the English High Court has confirmed the availability of website blocking as a remedy to tackle the problem of counterfeit and unauthorised semaglutide.

“This remedy is an innovative and powerful tool for taking effective action and forms an important part of the suite of enforcement remedies available against makers and sellers of unlawful products.”

data.title: Ozempic maker suffers boardroom exodus after row with shareholder
data.description:
The wealthy foundation behind Ozempic maker Novo Nordisk has seized control of the company in a boardroom coup after a row over the struggling group’s turnaround.

On Monday, the pharmaceutical giant said chairman Helge Lund and six independent directors would resign and be replaced by a group hand-picked by its biggest shareholder, the Novo Nordisk Foundation.

The drugmaker, which makes the anti-obesity jab Wegovy and Ozempic, said the decision came after a disagreement between the board and the foundation over a plan to rejuvenate the Danish company’s flagging fortunes.

The Novo Nordisk Foundation is the wealthiest charity in the world with £123bn in assets and the company’s largest shareholder, controlling 77pc of Novo.

The foundation has nominated five new directors to be appointed at an emergency meeting next month and a sixth to be appointed next year.

Lars Rebien Sørensen, the former chief executive of Novo, has been nominated to replace Mr Lund as chairman. Mr Lund recently left his position chairing BP after a disastrous attempt to focus the company on green energy.

Novo’s exodus amounts to the entire board, besides the foundation’s own investment boss and the four members elected by employees.

The rift between the company’s top shareholders and its board came to the fore after a slump in Novo’s performance.

The company makes the diabetes drugs Ozempic and Wegovy, which are used for weight loss. Both contain the ingredient semaglutide, which stimulates weight loss by making people feel full.

The initial success of the drugs led Novo to become Europe’s most valuable company, but shares have fallen by two thirds as it has faced competition from Eli Lilly, which makes rival drugs Mounjaro and Zepbound.

Novo’s own successor to Wegovy disappointed in weight loss trials, while the Trump administration has put pressure on the company to cut prices.

“Following dialogue with the Novo Nordisk Foundation regarding the future composition of the board of directors, it has not been possible to reach a common understanding,” Novo said.

“The board proposed a renewal focusing on [the] addition of select, new competencies while also maintaining continuity, whereas the board of the foundation wanted a more extensive reconfiguration.”

It said the new board’s first priority would be “implementing its transformation plans and regaining the company’s growth momentum”.

Mr Sørensen said: “Given the fast-moving environment in which Novo Nordisk operates, we believe that it is in the best interest of the company and its shareholders to implement a board renewal as soon as possible, rather than wait until the ordinary annual general meeting in March next year.”

He said the foundation fully supported the company’s new chief executive, Mike Doustdar, who replaced ousted boss Lars Fruergaard Jørgensen earlier this year, as well as recent plans to cut 9,000 jobs.

Novo’s shares fell by 2.4pc after the announcement.

data.title: How the Bank of England can secure its place at the heart of the next monetary revolution
data.description:
This morning, the Bank of England will set out its last consultation on a regime for the regulation of systemic stablecoins. How the UK pursues the regulation of digital money will determine whether London joins the US in leading the next era of financial infrastructure – or watches others set the pace. 

To its credit, the Bank’s position on the regulation of systemic stablecoins has evolved. Perhaps most remarkably, the Governor has explicitly acknowledged that the holding of money can be separated from the provision of credit – a profound shift in how we think about the structure of the current financial system. 

With the 180 day UK-US Taskforce for Markets of the Future, the UK has a rare opportunity to shape how innovation in money can preserve financial centre competitiveness.

The Governor has said that stablecoins aspiring to serve as money must be backed by “risk-free” assets. That principle is sound, but not one applied to traditional bank deposits. The Governor himself recognises that commercial bank deposits – with fractional backing – are not risk-free. 

In fact, stablecoins backed one-for-one by central bank cash reserves and other (credit) risk-free assets are less risky than uninsured deposits – and their regulatory treatment should reflect this.

The Bank’s willingness to allow a portion of reserves to be held in high quality liquid assets (HQLA) like gilts, rather than exclusively in central bank, deposits is welcome progress. A sensible approach would see a significant majority – perhaps over 80 per cent – in HQLA.

This has three advantages. First, it creates a viable pathway for issuers to transition between the FCA (non-systemic) and Bank of England (systemic) stablecoin regimes, without the need to fundamentally change business models. Second, it introduces new demand for short-dated gilts, supporting the UK Government’s funding base and lowering borrowing costs. Third, it aligns the UK regime with that of other major jurisdictions, making the country a more attractive destination for stablecoin innovation.

Concerns have been raised that in a crisis a run on a stablecoin issuer could trigger a fire-sale of gilts. If that’s the case, the Governor’s suggestion that the Bank could offer Emergency Liquidity Assistance (ELA) is one way to address this. 

The Bank also looks set to propose caps on stablecoin holdings, supposedly to guard against a “precipitous” drain of deposits from banks into digital money. 

Other jurisdictions offer a useful test case. In the United States, the dollar denominated stablecoin USDC has operated for nearly six years without evidence of a destabilising flight from retail deposits. In fact, in the 70 months of data from USDC, we observe that both retail deposits and USDC’s market cap have increased during that time. 

Empirical evidence suggests neither caps, nor a ban on stablecoins paying interest are justified. By proposing both, which no other jurisdiction has deemed necessary, the UK risks putting the brakes on innovation. 

The focus should be on facilitating modernisation, not privileging legacy systems. The Bank’s proposals sit awkwardly with both the Government’s competitiveness strategy and firms’ obligations under the Consumer Duty. 

Furthermore, it is unclear how caps on holdings would be implemented once a stablecoin transitions from the FCA’s regime for non-systemic stablecoins to the Bank of England’s regime once it becomes systemic - would there be forced liquidations? Second, the caps proposed are so low that it would make stablecoins unusable for wholesale settlement in tokenised markets. 

The rationale for caps is therefore weak, and industry needs clarity on when – and on what basis – those caps would be lifted. The Governor has said it is important to “think through the implications of change before going ahead”. But there is also risk in excessive caution. We should not want the UK to deliver a regulatory framework for stablecoins that deters innovation, at the same time as other markets move ahead.

The US has legislated to provide regulatory clarity on stablecoins, and the market has responded with around 80 different announcements of companies building in this space. 

The Bank’s evolving approach to stablecoins includes some strong and welcome progress. However, more can be done. In short, with prudence and ambition, a competitive, well-regulated stablecoin regime can strengthen financial stability and secure London’s place at the heart of the next monetary revolution. 

Faryar Shirzad is Chief Policy Officer at Coinbase

data.title: Drug giant hit by Trump autism claims bought for record $49bn
data.description:
A US drug giant hit by Donald Trump’s claims linking its paracetamol brand to an increased risk of autism has been bought for $49bn (£37bn) after shares crashed to a record low.

On Monday, Kenvue, which makes the Tylenol paracetamol brand, said it had agreed to be acquired by consumer goods giant Kimberly-Clark, which owns Kleenex, Huggies and Andrex.

The takeover comes after Kenvue shares hit their lowest level on record, with $13bn wiped off the company since the start of the year amid heightened scrutiny over Mr Trump’s claims about Tylenol.

In September, the US president said pregnant women should “fight like hell” not to take the paracetamol drug, urging them to “tough it out” when in pain.

Mr Trump went on to claim that the use of Tylenol during pregnancy could make autism more likely, citing unfounded links. The US president said: “Don’t take Tylenol. Don’t take it.”

The claim prompted a backlash from doctors who argue that it puts pregnant women’s health at risk.

The Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) issued guidance saying there was “no evidence that taking paracetamol during pregnancy causes autism in children”.

In the UK, Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, urged pregnant women not to pay “any attention whatsoever” to Mr Trump’s claims.

Kenvue said it “strongly disagrees with allegations that it does and is deeply concerned about the health risks and confusion this poses for expecting mothers and parents”.

Last week, Robert F Kennedy Jr, the US health secretary, also admitted that there was not “sufficient” evidence to link Tylenol to autism.

However, Kenvue is still facing a lawsuit in Texas over claims that it deceptively marketed its pain medication to pregnant people despite the alleged autism risk.

Kenvue’s $48.7bn takeover marks the largest buyout of a US consumer goods maker ever recorded, ahead of Mars’s $35.9bn takeover of Kellanova last year.

The offer price is at a 46pc premium to Kenvue’s closing price on Friday.

Kenvue, which was spun out of Johnson & Johnson in 2023, has also been drawn into court cases in the UK over its talc powder.

In October, a lawsuit was filed at the High Court over claims that Johnson & Johnson’s baby powder caused illnesses, including ovarian cancer, between 1965 and 2023.

Kenvue is fighting the claims, saying that it expects a judge to conclude its talc-based baby powder does not cause cancer.

The legal case led to claims that the scrutiny risked putting off potential buyers, with companies including Britain’s Haleon having previously been rumoured as possible buyers.

Kenvue kicked off a process to assess selling either parts or the whole company earlier this year in response to pressure from activist investors.

It ousted its chief executive in July as part of a push to prepare for a sale.

data.title: Rising energy bills expose Miliband’s fantasy economics
data.description:
The weather has turned very chilly. There is a thick frost on the ground, and a “beast from the east” may descend at any time.

Still, never mind. Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, promised us that he would take at least £300 off the average energy bill, so at least we can all afford to turn the heating up.

There is just one catch. We learnt on Friday that the energy price cap is going up yet again. In reality, the relentlessly rising cost of energy is exposing Miliband’s fantasy economics – and unless policy changes very soon, that is going to turn into a full-scale crisis.

Labour made very few promises during last year’s election campaign, but on two points it was completely clear. It would not raise any of the three main taxes, and it would cut average power bills by £300 per household.

We will find out next week whether the Chancellor Rachel Reeves can stick to the first one. But the second has already been completely shredded.

Instead of falling as expected, the energy regulator Ofgem surprised everyone by announcing that the price cap would rise again in January, just as the worst of the winter weather hits, and as a cost of living squeeze means that it is harder than ever to afford the cost of heating.

Seriously? Bills are going up again? In the background, the price of oil has been collapsing, falling to just $57 (£43.50) a barrel, down from $80 back in January. Wholesale energy prices have been going down, and are now 4pc lower over the last three months.

If there is a peace deal in Ukraine, prices might come down even further, while, of course, the massive investments the UK has been making in wind and solar power, plus all the money committed to GB Energy, should mean that domestic energy is now more plentiful than ever. And yet the average bill that the households will face is still going up.

In fairness, Ofgem is perfectly open about the reason for the increase. It “is driven by government policy costs and operating costs”, it admitted as it announced the increase.

The cost of building the Sizewell C nuclear power station will add around £1 to the average bill, while the Warm Home Discount will add an estimated 57p. 

The price rise is not driven by global prices, or even by domestic supply and demand. It is entirely the fault of Miliband.

There are two big problems with that. First, he completely misled everyone during the election. 

A reduction in energy bills was not a minor footnote or a passing remark that no one paid very much attention to. It was central to the Labour Party’s entire campaign at last year’s election, repeated again and again.

Despite that, it turns out that neither Miliband nor anyone else in his party had the faintest clue about how to deliver that once they were in power.

Next, and more seriously, his demented green policies are now directly pushing up prices. 

Expanding our nuclear capacity is a good idea, but the fact remains that cumbersome planning restrictions mean that building a new plant costs twice as much as it does in France and four times as much as in South Korea.

If we could build a new nuclear facility at the same price as other countries can, then prices would not need to rise by so much.

Likewise, the Warm Home Discount is just another form of welfare payment to lower income households, and while that might or might not be justifiable, it would surely be better to pay for that through general taxation instead of just whacking a bit more on everyone else’s bills.

It will get worse. The Government is pressing ahead with its “boiler tax”, which will mean extra costs for anyone who is unlucky enough to have their heating system pack up over the winter.

And it is doubling down on wind power, handing out lavish subsidies to the industry that will have to be paid for years to come, even as it becomes alarmingly obvious to everyone that wind is not nearly reliable enough.

The grid can’t cope with it, and costs are not coming under control in the way you would expect (indeed, it is looking as if solar is the far more cost effective option for green energy).

Meanwhile, we are still running down our oil and gas industry in the North Sea, even though there are plentiful resources left to supply our own needs.

In reality, Miliband has lost control of the energy market. Right now, the UK needs a complete reset.

That should mean two major changes. To start with, we need to drop the green targets. The UK has already more than halved its carbon emissions, and we account for less than 1pc of the global total.

At the rate we are deindustrialising – and ExxonMobil closed a major chemicals plant in Scotland only last week, accelerating the decline of manufacturing – it will soon be even lower. 

The priority right now is cheaper energy, not greener. It doesn’t matter whether it comes from wind, solar, gas, nuclear or even fossil fuels – it just needs to cost much, much less.

Next, we should drop the absurd system of price caps and controls. It is not helping anyone. We should have a plentiful supply of energy and then we should leave it to the market to decide which companies deliver it to people’s homes.

We may have only seen a modest rise this week. But no one should kid themselves about what is happening. 

Energy is getting more and more expensive, and the Government is to blame for that – and unless it changes course sooner or later we are all going to freeze to death.

data.title: Gulf royalty strikes deal to build thousands of London homes
data.description:
A Dubai-based property developer owned by Middle Eastern royalty has struck a deal to build thousands of homes alongside the River Thames.

Arada, co-founded by Sheikh Sultan bin Ahmed Al Qasimi of Sharjah, a city in the United Arab Emirates, and Saudi Arabia’s Prince Khaled bin Alwaleed bin Talal, has acquired an 80pc stake in Thameside West, a 47-acre development project in east London.

The scheme is set to deliver at least 5,000 homes near Royal Victoria Dock, and is estimated to be worth £2.5bn once completed.

It signals the growing influx of Middle Eastern money into London, particularly in the property sector.

Arada acquired the stake in Thameside West from London developer Keystone, paying an upfront fee of £225m alongside a pledge to invest a further £100m.

Keystone will own the remaining 20pc of the site, which is nearly twice the size of Hudson Yards in Manhattan, New York.

The acquisition is also one of the biggest land deals in London since 2020, giving the capital a much-needed boost after a recent slump in demand.

Just 5,933 new-build private homes were sold during the first three quarters of 2025, according to findings published by consultancy Molior London last month, with sales “weak across all local markets and at every price point”.

Dwindling sales have also led to a drop in construction, with activity hampered by high costs and uncertainty prompted by this month’s Budget.

Recent estimates show that just 15,000 to 20,000 private homes are to be under construction in London by the end of 2026.

‘Dubai model’

However, Ahmed Alkhoshaibi, the chief executive and co-founder of Arada, is confident that demand will recover.

“We will build and it will come,” he said. “That’s been the Dubai model. We will build even if there isn’t demand upfront.”

Mr Alkhoshaibi added that it is “the right time” to invest. He said: “It’s always the best time to buy when the price of the land is down as a developer.

“To create spaces that are on another level, you need to buy at the right price. Right now, London is down, but we’re confident it will be up sooner than later.”

It comes after Arada bought London housing developer Regal for £230m in September, with the aim of building 30,000 homes in the capital over the next three years.

During the 1990s, Prince Khaled was known for enjoying a lavish lifestyle, reportedly owning 200 luxury cars.

However, the guilt he experienced from taking part in a trophy hunt in South Africa in the latter part of the decade prompted him to adopt a more pared-back lifestyle – selling off his collection, becoming vegan and driving just one car, a Tesla.

His family also owns nearly 50pc of the Four Seasons international hotel brand, with the other half owned by Bill Gates.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Dutch back down in stand-off with China over chip factory seizure
data.description:
The Dutch government has backed down in a row for control of a crucial microchip manufacturer to China after a standoff triggered a crisis at European carmakers.

Vincent Karremans, the Dutch economic minister, said the Netherlands dropped an order seizing control of Nexperia to its Chinese owner Wingtech following negotiations with Beijing.

The Netherlands seized control of Nexperia in October, invoking a Cold War-era law, while its chief executive, Zhang Xuezheng, was ousted by a court.

A separate court decision in the Netherlands, that placed Wingtech’s shares in Nexperia under control of an independent administrator remains in effect, Nexperia said.

The Dutch government accused the company’s Chinese owners of “serious governance shortcomings” that threatened the supply of critical microchips to Europe.

According to Dutch media reports, officials also feared that Nexperia’s trade secrets had been stolen by China and it was planning to shift production out of Europe.

The move triggered a crisis in the European car industry, which is heavily reliant on Nexperia’s microchips.

China halted exports of chips to Europe while staff at the company’s Chinese division started refusing instructions from its headquarters on the continent.

Some of Europe’s biggest carmakers warned the supply chain shock could force them to shut their factories.

However, after an intervention this month by Donald Trump, the US president, China said it was prepared to ease an export ban on Nexperia’s technology.

On Tuesday, Mr Karremans said it was now the “right moment to take a constructive step by suspending my order under the Goods Availability Act regarding Nexperia”.

He said: “We are positive about the measures already taken by the Chinese authorities to ensure the supply of chips to Europe and the rest of the world. We see this as a show of goodwill. We will continue to engage in constructive dialogue with the Chinese authorities in the period ahead.”

The crisis has prompted calls for Labour to intervene at a Manchester chip plant that is also controlled by Nexperia.

Nexperia was previously forced to offload a factory it owned in Wales over national security concerns about its Chinese parent company.

Nexperia executives previously denied its ownership of the Welsh plant presented a national security risk. It did not comment on the Manchester claims.

Sir Iain Duncan Smith, a former Conservative Party leader, told The Telegraph last month: “The same rules should apply. I don’t understand why this hasn’t been resolved by the Government.”

A Wingtech spokesman said the Dutch intervention in Nexperia had threatened the “continuity of Nexperia and therefore the economic security of the Netherlands and Europe – which is the exact same argument the Dutch government made previously in support of judicial intervention”.

The spokesman added: “Minister Karremans justified his actions by accusing Nexperia’s CEO of various acts of alleged mismanagement.

“Wingtech strongly rejects these accusations and points out that, to date, no proof has been provided.”

data.title: Christmas delivery delay fears grow as UPS staff threaten to strike
data.description:
Christmas gift deliveries are at risk of delays as 2,000 UPS workers threaten to go on strike.

Union Unite claimed the public would face major delays to festive online shopping deliveries and gifts sent using the service as UPS staff, including delivery drivers, consider walking out.

The union warned that Apple customers in particular could see delays as the tech giant usually uses UPS as its courier. Amazon, Dell and Louis Vuitton are also expected to be affected. A source close to Amazon said UPS handled only a small proportion of its UK parcels.

Staff are being balloted on industrial action in a dispute over pay. If approved, the walkouts would begin in mid-December after it opened the ballot this week. It will close the vote on Wednesday Dec 3.

Union members rejected an offer from UPS of a 2.8pc pay rise this year, followed by 3.2pc in 2026. They said the package was less than the rate of inflation, which rose to 3.6pc in October.

Sharon Graham, the Unite general secretary, said: “This dispute is a clear case of a rich employer putting profits before people.

“It is shameful that workers at a company that turns over billions have been offered such a miserly pay deal and have been left struggling to get by.

“Our members at UPS have Unite’s full support in their fight for a fair pay rise.”

‘Prioritising its own greed’

The strike action at UPS, headquartered in Feltham, west London, has echoes of a Royal Mail strike that disrupted deliveries during the Christmas of 2022. The chaos led to a surge in returned presents after packages arrived too late to be gifted.

As well as demanding higher pay, UPS workers said they had been forced to work overtime and been given “unreasonable workloads” after the world’s biggest parcel courier failed to replace staff.

Unite added there were fears around job security driven by the use of outsourcing.

Matt Draper, Unite’s national officer, said: “Any industrial action and disruption to customers will be entirely the fault of UPS, which is prioritising its own greed.

“Management need to come back to the table with a pay offer that is acceptable to our members as well as commit to improving conditions.”

A UPS spokesman said: “We endeavour to create a working environment that helps our people to thrive and our business and customers to succeed. Our goal remains a balanced, fair and sustainable agreement that reflects the valuable contribution our people deliver every day.

“Robust contingency plans are in place to minimise any customer disruption and keep our commitments should industrial action take place.”

data.title: Former British insurance boss loses top job over ‘workplace affair’
data.description:
A former City boss has lost out on a £10m-a-year job at one of America’s top insurance companies over claims he had an affair with a colleague.

John Neal, who ran Lloyd’s of London until earlier this year, was set to join New York insurer AIG but had his offer revoked last week after the US giant learned he was being investigated for an alleged past workplace romance.

Lloyd’s, the centuries-old insurance marketplace, is looking into claims Mr Neal had a relationship with Rebekah Clement, a PR executive at the company, and unfairly promoted her.

Ms Clement, a former press secretary to New Zealand’s prime minister, was hired as a senior communications manager by Lloyd’s in 2015 before being promoted to a newly-created position of corporate affairs director in 2023, reporting directly to Mr Neal.

At the time, Mr Neal praised her “experience” and suggested it could help Lloyd’s “build stronger relationships”.

Staff are said to have claimed Ms Clement was given preferential treatment because of Mr Neal was in a relationship with her, the Wall Street Journal reported.

Mr Neal left Lloyd’s in May but the probe was only launched last month after Sir Charles Roxburgh, Lloyd’s chairman, became aware of “market speculation concerning possible historic breaches of policy”, Lloyd’s said.

Sheila Cameron, chief of the Lloyd’s Market Association, which represents members of the marketplace, welcomed the launch of the investigation.

She said members “will be as appalled as we are at the possibility of the market being tarnished by alleged poor behaviours from a small minority of leaders, who were previously at the top of Lloyd’s”.

Mr Neal has found himself in trouble for workplace romances in the past. He previously had his bonus cut in 2017 for failing to disclose a relationship with his executive assistant while running Australian insurer QBE.

On leaving Lloyd’s, Mr Neal had been set to join American insurance giant AIG as second-in-command. However, his appointment was abruptly cancelled last week with the company citing “personal reasons”.

Mr Neal was in line to be paid $14.2m (£10m) a year in his new position, far more than the roughly £1m he took home during his final year at Lloyd’s.

Ms Clement left Lloyd’s in May, shortly after Mr Neal stepped down.

Founded in 1689, Lloyd’s of London is an insurance market that lets its members broker new insurance policies for everything from cargo ships to oil rigs.

It emerged out of the City of London coffee houses that were once frequented by merchants and shipowners, growing into the biggest insurance marketplace in the world. Today it is based inside central London’s inside-out building, designed Sir Norman Foster and opened in 1986.

The investigation follows a string of scandals around workplace affairs at some of the world’s largest companies.

Bernard Looney, the former chief of BP, stepped down in September 2023 after failing to disclose relationships with colleagues.

Steve Easterbrook, the chief executive of McDonald’s, was fired from his job in 2019 over a romance he had with one of his employees.

Mr Neal and Ms Clement were contacted for comment.

data.title: Best easy-access accounts: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
In their purest form, easy-access accounts provide straightforward access to your cash. They can be the simplest type of savings account to hold, and are a good idea if you want to regularly deposit and withdraw funds.

Easy-access accounts usually have variable interest rates, which can be increased and decreased at the provider’s discretion – but it should always let you know before the rate changes.

However, these accounts can come with a range of terms that make them more complicated. For instance, some include introductory bonus rates, which are essentially temporary offers to attract new customers. They’re usually good deals when you first sign up, but you’ll need to keep an eye on when they end and might want to switch as soon as they do.

Some accounts also have limited access – for instance, only allowing a certain number of withdrawals each year. If you exceed these, you may be punished with a lower interest rate or the account may automatically close. It’s worth checking the terms to make sure you’re clear on how the account works before you sign up.

In this guide, Telegraph Money explains:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best easy-access savings accounts for November 2025
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing an easy-access savings account
  • Variable interest vs fixed-rate
  • Easy access account FAQs

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, such as those that require minimum deposits of more than £25,000 or are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS), which covers up to £85,000 per person, per financial institution. The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

Best easy-access savings accounts for November 2025

See the table below to see the current top-rate easy-access savings accounts.

There are lots of other accounts that will let you access your cash whenever you want to. See our guides to the best current accounts that pay interest, the best regular accounts and best notice accounts – not to mention our guide to the best cash Isas, which lists out a range of the top tax-free accounts.

For options on the top rates when you lock your cash away for a year or more, see our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing an easy-access savings account

Caitlyn Eastell, of Moneyfactscompare.co.uk, said: “Easy-access accounts are useful for keeping an emergency fund to cover any unexpected costs, as they allow savers to dip in and out when needed.”

But there are downsides, she added: “As the money isn’t locked away, it may be more tempting for savers to unnecessarily withdraw cash.

“The interest rates on easy-access accounts are variable, so could drop at short notice. In some cases, the number of withdrawals may be limited and, if exceeded, may cause the rate to drop dramatically.”

Mark Hicks, of Hargreaves Lansdown, added: “Easy-access accounts are probably the most popular in the market at the moment, offering good rates.

“However, clients do need to be aware that this rate is not guaranteed. It is important to monitor the easy-access product as quite often clients forget and then a year later, realise the rate is not attractive.”

Variable interest vs fixed-rate

Easy access accounts are almost always variable, which means the interest rate can change at any time. Fixed-rate accounts, as the name suggests, keep the same rate for the duration of the fixed term.

Both have pros and cons, depending on what’s happening with the savings market. Signing up to a fixed rate can shield you in times where rates are on the way down and, to compensate for the fact that most of these accounts don’t allow withdrawals during the fixed term, the rates they offer are usually higher.

However, in times when savings rates are rising, having your cash stuck in a fixed rate account can mean you’ll miss out on higher interest elsewhere.

Easy access account FAQs

While instant-access and easy-access accounts are very similar, the slight difference is that instant-access accounts allow you to make withdrawals directly from the account, with no limit on how much you can withdraw or when you can take money out.

An easy-access account can include some restrictions, such as those we’ve mentioned above. Easy-access accounts might also have daily withdrawal limits and you might need to transfer the cash to a current account before you can actually spend it.

It’s generally accepted that you should have between three and six months’ worth of living expenses saved to fall back on in an emergency. If you’re retired, this increases to one to three years’ worth. It’s good to hold this money in an easy-access account that allows you to withdraw quickly, whenever you might need it.

If you have larger sums that you don’t need access to, you might find a better rate by putting it in a fixed-rate bond, or you might consider investing.

The minimum balance for an easy-access account can vary hugely, and it’s not uncommon to see accounts requiring anything from as little as £1 up to £10,000. Shop around for the account which best suits your needs and budget.

data.title: Inflation calculator: how the latest rate will affect your savings
data.description:
This calculator shows the current rate of Consumer Prices Index (CPI) inflation will affect the return on your savings.

Inflation calculator

How to use this calculator

Type in the amount you have in a savings account or cash Isa, and the interest rate you’re currently receiving.

Press the “Calculate” button to see the result.

How it works

This calculator works by figuring out how much interest your savings will earn over the course of a year – this assumes interest is paid annually, and the interest rate and amount of savings remains the same.

This is pitted against the most recent CPI measure of inflation, as reported by the Office for National Statistics.

Assuming inflation remains steady, it then shows the value your savings will lose in real terms due to inflationary price increases.

The calculator will also show you what the cumulative impact of these losses will be for each of the next five years.

What is this calculator useful for?

The calculator can help you gauge whether or not it’s worth switching to a savings account or cash Isa offering a higher rate of interest.

This is particularly important when inflation is high, but in general it’s a good idea to make sure you’re taking advantage of the best savings rates to reduce the effect of price increases. If you don’t, put simply, your money won’t buy as much as it could the previous year.

You can also use the calculator to see how much of a difference it would make to switch savings accounts for a better rate.

For example, someone with £20,000 in an account paying today’s average easy-access rate, according to Moneyfacts, of 2.51pc would make a “real terms” loss of £210.42 after a year because of inflation. They would earn £502 in interest, but their pot would effectively be worth just £19,789.58 after a year when adjusted for inflation.

Switching to the top easy-access savings rate which, according to today’s tables showing the best easy access savings accounts, is from Cahoot and pays 5pc, they’d make an inflationary increase of £270.27, based on actual interest of £1,000.
