data.title: Youth unemployment is hitting sales of trainers, says JD Sports
data.description:
Youth unemployment is damaging sales of trainers, JD Sports has warned.

The sportswear retail giant signalled that cash-strapped young shoppers risk struggling to afford new shoes after being pushed out of work by Labour’s National Insurance tax raid.

After reporting a 3.3pc drop in like-for-like sales between September and November, JD Sports sounded the alarm over “rising unemployment levels” among its core customers, who are typically aged between 16 and 24.

JD Sports said it was particularly worried by weaker data concerning the economy and consumer confidence.

It said it was expecting profits to be at the lower end of expectations of between £853m and £888m for the year to the beginning of February.

The latest warning follows a recent surge in the number of young people out of work, as they have been hit by companies scaling back hiring and cutting jobs in the past year.

Official figures on Thursday revealed that almost one million people aged between 16 and 24 were not in education, employment or training (Neets) in the three months to September.

This figure has been rising over the past three years. Since 2022, the number of people aged 16 to 24 who are Neets has risen from 740,000 to 946,000.

Pat McFadden, the Work and Pensions Secretary, this week warned that this up-tick threatened to lead to a life on benefits for many young people.

A recent review into the worklessness crisis found that if a 22-year-old falls out of work for health reasons, they could end up more than £1m worse off over their lifetime than if they had been employed.

However, many large employers have been cutting entry-level roles in response to soaring labour costs and red tape.

It comes after Rachel Reeves launched a £25bn raid on employers’ National Insurance contributions (NICs) in her maiden Budget.

This saw the Chancellor increase the NICS rate from 13.8pc to 15pc and lower the threshold at which it is paid from £9,100 to £5,000 at her last Budget.

Companies are also reining in hiring ahead of new regulation.

This relates to the upcoming workers’ rights bill, which will include giving employees a day-one right against unfair dismissal.

Retail chiefs have warned that this will further dampen hiring.

Régis Schultz, the JD Sports chief executive, said the company was “controlling what we can”. It typically relies heavily on big sportswear brands, which tend to sell products at higher prices. Around 50pc of its revenue comes from Nike.

Mr Schultz added: “As we enter an important trading period, we are mindful of recent weak macro and consumer indicators in our key markets.”

On Thursday, the British Retail Consortium said consumer confidence had plunged in recent weeks amid fears around the upcoming Budget.

data.title: Marmite, Bovril and Colman’s up for sale
data.description:
Marmite, Bovril and Colman’s Mustard could change hands for the first time in 25 years as the owner of the iconic British brands considers a sale.

Unilever is mulling a carve-off of some of its best-known British products as it pivots from food to health and beauty. The FTSE 100 consumer goods giant owns brands including Dove, TRESemmé and Vaseline.

A sale risks being seen as a further retreat from Britain by the Anglo-Dutch business. Unilever sold off Scottish-founded Liptons Tea to private equity in 2022 and has snubbed the London Stock Exchange for a looming spin-off of its ice cream brands.

Marmite, Bovril and Colman’s Mustard are some of the UK’s most distinguished food brands and collectively bring in sales of £200m a year, according to Reuters, which first reported the possible sale.

All three brands have been owned by Unilever for more than 20 years. A sale would open up the possibility of the brands falling into foreign ownership.

Unilever has been offloading brands as part of a turnaround drive.

Last year, Hein Schumacher, the company’s then-chief executive, said Unilever needed to slim down its “rather eclectic portfolio of food brands”. He kicked off a spin-off of Unilever’s €15bn (£12.5bn) Magnum ice cream business.

Rivals have also been offloading assets. Nestle is looking to sell its bottled water business, while Kraft Heinz is also planning to break itself up.

Colman’s is a Norwich success story that has been around since 1814. Unilever, which has owned the brand for three decades, sparked controversy when it moved production of the mustard away from East Anglia in 2019, following 200 years at the factory, to Staffordshire.

Unilever has owned Marmite, the yeast extract spread, and Bovril since 2000, picking them up during its $20bn (£15bn) takeover of US giant Bestfoods.

Both brands are much-loved British staples.

Marmite was founded in Burton-on-Trent in 1902, utilising leftover yeast from the Bass brewery. It later went on to be part of soldiers’ ration packs during the First World War because of its high nutritional value.

Bovril was created by Scotsman John Lawson Johnston in 1871 with the aim of sustaining Napoleon’s army when access to meat was scarce.

As part of the Bestfoods deal in 2000, Unilever also gained ownership of a host of other top-selling brands including Hellmann’s mayonnaise and Ambrosia desserts. 

Hellmann’s – a New York brand – is not expected to be included in a sale, as it is among Unilever’s top 30 “power brands”. Pot Noodle, another British brand, is also expected to be retained.

Mr Schumacher was ousted in February this year after struggling to convince shareholders of his revival efforts. Its shares have remained largely stagnant since 2017.

He was replaced by Fernando Fernández, who has pledged to speed up a turnaround push.

Last month, reports suggested he was plotting to sell a number of European food brands, worth between $1bn to $1.5bn.

Consumer goods giants like Unilever have been battling with squeezed profit margins and stagnant sales as high inflation hits customers and their own factories.

Unilever has also been fighting to regain momentum after what critics claimed was a misguided attempt to embed ethics into its business.

Former chief Alan Jope, who left in 2023, declared that all its brands needed to have “purpose”. In 2019, he pledged to sell off brands that “are not able to stand for something more important than just making your hair shiny, your skin soft, your clothes whiter or your food tastier”.

Influential investor Terry Smith subsequently said the company had “lost the plot”.

He wrote at the time: “The Hellmann’s brand has existed since 1913 so we would guess that by now consumers have figured out its purpose (spoiler alert – salads and sandwiches).”

Unilever declined to comment.

data.title: Best regular savings accounts: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
A regular savings account requires you to commit to paying in a certain amount of money each month, usually between £10 and £500. In return, the bank gives you a higher interest rate than you would normally get with other types of accounts.

Regular savers are usually fixed-term accounts which last for a year.

In this guide, Telegraph money will cover:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best regular savings accounts
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a regular savings account
  • Regular savings FAQs

How we determine the best rates

Telegraph Money’s Best Buy tables show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that require minimum deposits of more than £25,000 or are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme. The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best regular savings account

Use the table below to discover the best regular savings accounts for you.

For other savings options that allow you to access your money, see our guides to the best easy-access savings accounts, along with the best notice accounts and best current accounts.

You might be able to find higher rates by locking your cash away – don’t miss our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

Plus, don’t miss our guide to the best cash Isas, which shows the top options for easy-access, notice, regular and fixed-rates – where your savings interest is all tax-free.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a regular savings account

Mark Hicks, of investment and savings platform Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “Regular savers can have a very catchy headline rate. But quite often, the maximum amount of money you can deposit is low. Banks may attract you in with a high interest rate and then you realise you can save £5,000.”

“They are very good if you want to deposit small amounts of money and if you’re new to saving,” he added.

And Caitlyn Eastell, of Moneyfactscompare.co.uk said: “Regular savings accounts are an ideal choice for those wanting to receive a higher rate of interest or wish to develop and maintain a regular savings habit.

“However, many regular savings accounts don’t give much flexibility on the amount that can be deposited each month and often have minimum or maximum monthly payments.”

Regular savings FAQs

Yes, you can open more than one account. Regular savers are for making smaller payments, so there is nothing stopping you from opening several to earn more interest on your money.

Some accounts will have a minimum number of payments you need to make each year – often 10 or 11. Some will require you to pay in a certain amount every month.

Yes, money held in regular savings accounts is protected as long as your account is with a UK-regulated bank or building society, the money is protected under the Financial Services Compensation Scheme up to £85,000 per institution.

Yes, you can open a regular savings account for your children. Check our guide to the best children’s savings accounts to see the latest rates.

It takes time to build up the amount of money you have in the account because you can only make small deposits.

It is also worth bearing in mind that the interest you get will be less than the headline rate. This is how it works when the money is saved monthly, rather than one lump sum.

A regular savings account can help develop good saving habits. If you are only able to put a small amount of money away each month and would prefer a short-term arrangement, then it might be the best situation for you.

A regular savings account gives you considerably better interest rates than standard accounts, rewarding you for making frequent savings over a year or so.

However, there are generally rigid terms and conditions. The amount you can save is capped and your interest rate might be reduced if you don’t save every month or if you need to make a withdrawal. Many accounts don’t allow early withdrawals whatsoever.

You usually need to have another product with the same bank – usually a current account.

The rate generally lasts for a year, meaning your money is moved to an account with lower interest rates at the end of the term.

Some accounts have variable interest rates – meaning the provider can change the rates whenever they want – so it’s important to keep an eye on messages from the provider and switch, if the rates drop too low.

Any tax due on interest you earn over the personal savings allowance is generally calculated by the bank. Rachel Springall, of Moneyfactscompare.co.uk, said: “Banks will notify HMRC [HM Revenue & Customs] of the annual interest received and consumers’ records will be updated based on this. Consumers can contact HMRC by telephone or in writing before this is done. Any charges will be notified by way of a calculation and tax code change.”

“There are many savers out there who have been vocal to not want their tax code changed, so they would need to pay HMRC a lump sum, and thus it may be beneficial for them to proactively contact HMRC with their details,” she added.

data.title: Steve Reed lines up administrators for Thames Water
data.description:
Ministers have appointed City insolvency advisers to prepare for the potential collapse of Thames Water.

Steve Reed, the Environment Secretary, has selected FTI Consulting to help draw up contingency plans in the event that the struggling utility giant is placed into a taxpayer-backed special administration regime.

It comes as Thames Water, which has 16 million customers across London and the South East, teeters on the brink of collapse as it struggles under a £17bn debt pile.

Its future was cast into fresh doubt in June after private equity firm KKR pulled out of a £4bn rescue deal.

The company’s creditors have put forward an alternative £17bn rescue plan, but the bailout is contingent on regulators agreeing to waive historical fines.

Mr Reed has previously suggested this would not happen, making a government intervention appear increasingly likely. It is estimated that a state bailout would cost the taxpayer as much as £4bn.

The Environment Secretary in June said Labour was stepping up preparations to temporarily nationalise Thames Water. A government source said the move was a natural consequence of this.

The move to appoint FTI, which was first reported by Sky News, places the consulting firm in prime position to act as the company’s administrator should it collapse into insolvency, though the appointment of a special administrator is ultimately a decision for the court.

A Government spokesman said: “The Government will always act in the national interest on these issues.

“The company remains financially stable, but we have stepped up our preparations and stand ready for all eventualities, including applying for a special administration regime if that were to become necessary.”

Thames is currently locked in discussions with its creditors and regulator Ofwat over a new rescue deal for the company.

Creditors have been pushing for ministers to intervene to wipe out historical fines, saying a “regulatory reset” was required to ensure the utility is financially sustainable.

Thames was handed a record £123m fine in May and creditors say it could be in line for penalties of more than £1bn relating to environmental and pollution failings.

The £17bn rescue plan involves injecting £5bn in new equity and debt and writing off billions of pounds in existing debt.

A Thames Water spokesman said: “Our focus remains on a holistic and fundamental recapitalisation, delivering a market-led solution which includes targeting investment grade credit ratings and returning the company to a stable financial foundation.

“Constructive discussions with our many stakeholders continue.”

data.title: Britain’s Victorian railway embankments are collapsing. And it could cost us billions
data.description:
When a London-bound express train derailed near the village of Shap in Cumbria earlier this month after ploughing into a landslide, ministers insisted that such incidents were vanishingly rare.

What they failed to mention was that landslips on the railway have reached an all-time high, as a stormier and rainier climate threatens to damage hundreds of miles of embankments dating back to Victorian times.

With Britain’s trains busier than ever, fears are now growing that these centuries-old railway embankments cutting through the countryside could pose a serious risk to passengers – and cost billions to make safer.

Britain’s creaking railways have many problems – from overcrowding and late timetables to poor Wi-Fi and constant signal failures. Yet the dangers posed by landslides and poorly maintained embankments can have fatal consequences.

In 2020, three people died in Stonehaven, Scotland when the lead carriages of a high-speed train en route to Aberdeen plunged down a bank after hitting a landslide triggered by torrential rain.

The accident was the first to kill a passenger since 2007 and the first fatal crash caused by a train hitting a landslip since 1995.

Although last month’s Cumbria derailment was the first major landslide incident since Stonehaven, there are concerns that Britain’s railway system is poorly equipped to detect landslides and prevent further derailments.

Alarmingly, only a minority of landslides are detected by monitors set up to provide a warning when they occur.

Just 8pc of landslips – 13 of 147 – were detected via remote sensors last year, despite £33m having been spent on monitors, a freedom of information request to the Office of Rail and Road found.

In the case of the Cumbria derailment, the Rail Accident Investigation Branch (RAIB) has said that one of its chief concerns was the “effectiveness of on-site monitoring equipment”.

The spike in incidents and low detection rates has begun to raise concerns about whether remote monitoring can protect passengers – and the costs of upgrading the system if not.

Currently, the vast majority of incidents are reported by train drivers, maintenance crews and engineers.

And drivers are becoming increasingly concerned about the growing risks from landslides, some railway chiefs say.

Tricia Williams, managing director of Northern, Britain’s second-biggest train operator, said after Cumbria she was forced to reassure her drivers and train crew that it was safe for services to operate across the north of England.

Ms Williams said Northern had suffered its own near miss at Grange-over-Sands in Cumbria when the track collapsed ahead of a train as an embankment was washed away.

“We have to recognise it’s a risk, but we’ve got to keep the railway moving. The key is the monitoring equipment,” she said.

“I think there was a monitor at Shap, but there needs to be a certain number of monitors triggered. The message to my team is that they are the eyes and ears as well, and that if they see things that they are worried about, report them.”

Expensive alternative

The alternative to driver monitoring is the saturation of the entire 10,000-mile rail network with remote sensors.

But coupled with measures to shore up thousands of miles of slopes, that could cost billions.

Gareth Evans, head of Rail Technology at Network Rail, said such an approach would be so expensive as to be impractical.

He said: “We have to take a risk-based approach to understanding the earthwork assets because there’s so many of them it would not be affordable for the taxpayer to deploy instruments on every single metre.”

The Aslef train drivers’ union said that, whatever the bill, everything possible must be done to enhance safety and avoid a repeat of Stonehaven.

Mick Whelan, Aslef general secretary, said: “Stonehaven casts a long shadow and we must ensure that such an accident never happens in this country again.

“While we understand that there are many cuttings, embankments, bridges, and viaducts on Britain’s railway network, it is vital that Network Rail has the money to ensure that landslips, and other failures, do not happen.”

In the recent Cumbria incident, an Avanti Pendolino tilting train ran into a mudslide immediately south of a summit near Shap, which is the highest point on the West Coast Main Line in England.

While the train was operating at only 83mph, two-thirds of its top speed, and the landslide was shallow – estimates suggest a depth of just a few feet – the impact was sufficient to lift the wheels of the leading carriage off the rails and into the gap between the tracks.

It then ran for more than half a kilometre before coming to a standstill. Extensive damage was caused to the train and track, though just four of the 95 people on board were hurt.

At the Pendolino’s top speed of 125mph, and with a full load of 600-plus passengers, however, the outcome could have been very different.

The RAIB’s preliminary report said a drainage ditch has been flooded because of the heavy rain, leading the slope of the embankment to become too muddy and causing the landslide.

Yet, worryingly, monitors were in operation in the area but seemingly failed to detect the landslip. A Network Rail spokesman declined to comment further on concerns regarding monitoring equipment.

Sensors failure

Network Rail, which is due to be merged with nationalised train operators to create Great British Railways (GBR), is responsible for more than 190,000 individual “earthworks” – which include embankments and soil and rock cuttings.

Rock cuttings are the most likely to suffer some kind of landslide, but they can be covered up to catch any falling stones.

It is soil cuttings that have become the main focus of monitoring, which over 20 years has progressed from visual inspections through instruments drilled into the ground to lower-cost sensor technologies that directly alert rail control rooms to any ground movement.

Simon Abbott, Network Rail head of geotechnics, said that in cases where “sensors are taken out by a large failure” there are many examples of them “having worked really well”.

However, he said that monitoring is “not a silver bullet”.

“Sending people out is a resource we don’t really have at our disposal any more, so technology is playing more of a part,” he said.

Mr Abbott added: “You could put more investment into any aspect of that overall approach and see value, but it’s how you use an appropriate level of funding across the spectrum.

“We’ve got a huge amount of work that we’ve identified where we can justify intervening, and we’ve also got sites where we want to strengthen how we manage risk.”

Network Rail has also placed greater focus on weather forecasting in the wake of Stonehaven, with trains drastically slowed down in areas experiencing heavy rain.

Following the recent Cumbria incident, Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, pledged that safety would remain the number one priority for the industry through nationalisation.

She said: “It is right that Network Rail are making sure they shape the way in which they are spending money to address the changing climate.

“I do want proper monitoring to be in place to make sure that we reduce disruption to a minimum and that we can keep our railways operating as safely as possible.

“I don’t ever want to be in a position again where I’m doing a broadcast round and find out that there has been a train derailment.”

However, Richard Holden, the Conservative transport spokesman, said he is concerned that Labour’s creation of GBR will erect barriers to the work needed to combat landslides.

He said: “We want to see more and better monitoring, but you get that through innovation. And to get the Treasury to invest in innovation you’ve got to have a growing rail sector.

“You need a railway which is operating busily so that the risk of not doing something can be calculated in financial terms. If stopping a popular service running is going to involve a huge amount of cost, it’s going to get national attention, as the Shap incident did.

“But as the railway is brought under state control, who is going to be fighting for resources at a time when the Government is being squeezed on multiple different fronts?”
